**British Columbia**[[b]](#cite_note-8) is the westernmost [province of Canada](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada"). Situated in the [Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest") between the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") and the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains"), the province has a diverse geography, with rugged landscapes that include rocky coastlines, sandy beaches, forests, lakes, mountains, inland deserts, and grassy plains.[[7]](#cite_note-9) British Columbia borders the province of [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") to the east; the territories of [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon") and [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories") to the north; the US states of [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"), and [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") to the south; and [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") to the northwest. With an estimated population of 5.68 million as of 2025, it is Canada's [third-most populous province](/wiki/Population_of_Canada_by_province_and_territory "Population of Canada by province and territory"). The capital of British Columbia is [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia"), while the province's largest city is [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"). Vancouver and its suburbs together make up [the third-largest metropolitan area in Canada](/wiki/List_of_census_metropolitan_areas_and_agglomerations_in_Canada "List of census metropolitan areas and agglomerations in Canada"), with the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") recording 2.6 million people in [Metro Vancouver](/wiki/Metro_Vancouver_Regional_District "Metro Vancouver Regional District").[[8]](#cite_note-10) British Columbia is Canada's third-largest province in terms of total area, after [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") and [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario").[[9]](#cite_note-11)

The [first known](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") human inhabitants of the area settled in British Columbia at least 10,000 years ago. Such groups include the [Coast Salish](/wiki/Coast_Salish "Coast Salish"), [Tsilhqotʼin](/wiki/Tsilhqot%CA%BCin "Tsilhqotʼin"), and [Haida](/wiki/Haida_people "Haida people") peoples, among many others. One of the earliest British settlements in the area was [Fort Victoria](/wiki/Fort_Victoria_(British_Columbia) "Fort Victoria (British Columbia)"), established in 1843, which gave rise to the city of Victoria, the capital of the [Colony of Vancouver Island](/wiki/Colony_of_Vancouver_Island "Colony of Vancouver Island"). The [Colony of British Columbia (1858–1866)](/wiki/Colony_of_British_Columbia_(1858%E2%80%931866) "Colony of British Columbia (1858–1866)") was subsequently founded by [Richard Clement Moody](/wiki/Richard_Clement_Moody "Richard Clement Moody"),[[10]](#cite_note-Richard_Clement_Moody_Obituary,_ICE-12) and by the [Royal Engineers, Columbia Detachment](/wiki/Royal_Engineers,_Columbia_Detachment "Royal Engineers, Columbia Detachment"), in response to the [Fraser Canyon Gold Rush](/wiki/Fraser_Canyon_Gold_Rush "Fraser Canyon Gold Rush"). Moody selected the site for and founded the mainland colony's capital [New Westminster](/wiki/New_Westminster "New Westminster").[[11]](#cite_note-Edward_1887_215_in_New_Westminster_District_Directory-13)[[12]](#cite_note-DFB_Richard_Clement_Moody-14)[[13]](#cite_note-DNB-15) The colonies of Vancouver Island and British Columbia were incorporated in 1866, subsequent to which Victoria became the [united colony](/wiki/Colony_of_British_Columbia_(1866%E2%80%931871) "Colony of British Columbia (1866–1871)")'s capital. In 1871, British Columbia entered [Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") as the sixth province of Canada, in enactment of the *[British Columbia Terms of Union](/wiki/British_Columbia_Terms_of_Union "British Columbia Terms of Union")*.

British Columbia is a diverse and cosmopolitan province, drawing on a plethora of cultural influences from its [diasporas](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora") of [British](/wiki/British_Canadians "British Canadians"), [European](/wiki/European_Canadians "European Canadians"), and [Asian Canadians](/wiki/Asian_Canadians "Asian Canadians"), as well as the [Indigenous population](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Pacific_Northwest_Coast "Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast"). Though the province's ethnic majority originates from the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), many British Columbians also trace their ancestors to [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"), [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia").[[14]](#cite_note-2016CensusBCEthnicOrigin-16) [Indigenous Canadians](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada") constitute about 6 percent of the province's total population.[[15]](#cite_note-indigenouspopulation2021-17) Christianity is the largest religion in the region, though the majority of the population is non-religious.[[16]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-19) English is the common language of the province, although [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese"), and [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") also have a large presence in the Metro Vancouver region. The [Franco-Columbian](/wiki/Franco-Columbian "Franco-Columbian") community is an officially recognized linguistic minority, and around one percent of British Columbians claim [French as their mother tongue](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians").[[18]](#cite_note-2016CensusBCLanguage-20) British Columbia is home to at least 34 distinct [Indigenous languages](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "Indigenous languages of the Americas").[[19]](#cite_note-21)

Major sectors of [British Columbia's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_British_Columbia "Economy of British Columbia") include [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry"), mining, filmmaking and [video production](/wiki/Video_production "Video production"), tourism, real estate, construction, wholesale, and retail. Its main exports include [lumber](/wiki/Lumber "Lumber") and timber, [pulp and paper](/wiki/Pulp_and_paper "Pulp and paper") products, copper, coal, and natural gas.[[20]](#cite_note-22) British Columbia exhibits [high property values](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate") and is a significant centre for [maritime trade](/wiki/Maritime_trade "Maritime trade"):[[21]](#cite_note-23) the [Port of Vancouver](/wiki/Port_of_Vancouver "Port of Vancouver") is the largest port in Canada and the most diversified port in North America.[[22]](#cite_note-24) Although less than 5 percent of the province's territory is [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land"), significant agriculture exists in the [Fraser Valley](/wiki/Fraser_Valley "Fraser Valley") and [Okanagan](/wiki/Okanagan "Okanagan") due to the warmer climate.[[23]](#cite_note-25) British Columbia is home to 45% of all publicly listed companies in Canada.[[24]](#cite_note-26)

## Etymology

The name of the province was chosen by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"), when the [Colony of British Columbia (1858–1866)](/wiki/Colony_of_British_Columbia_(1858%E2%80%931866) "Colony of British Columbia (1858–1866)"), i.e., "the Mainland", became a British colony in 1858.[[25]](#cite_note-27) It refers to the [Columbia District](/wiki/Columbia_District "Columbia District"), the British name for the territory drained by the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River"), in southeastern British Columbia, which was the namesake of the [Columbia Department](/wiki/Columbia_Department "Columbia Department") of the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company"). Queen Victoria chose *British* Columbia to distinguish what was the British sector of the Columbia District from the United States' ("American Columbia" or "Southern Columbia"), which became the [Oregon Territory](/wiki/Oregon_Territory "Oregon Territory") on August 8, 1848, as a result of the treaty.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

Ultimately, the *Columbia* in the name *British Columbia* is derived from the name of the *[Columbia Rediviva](/wiki/Columbia_Rediviva "Columbia Rediviva")*, an American ship which lent its name to the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River") and later the wider region;[[27]](#cite_note-29) the *Columbia* in the name *Columbia Rediviva* came from [the name *Columbia* for the New World or parts thereof](/wiki/Columbia_(name) "Columbia (name)"), a reference to [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus").

The governments of Canada and British Columbia recognize *Colombie-Britannique* as the French name for the province.[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of British Columbia](/wiki/Geography_of_British_Columbia "Geography of British Columbia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/BC_Elevation.svg/500px-BC_Elevation.svg.png)](/wiki/File:BC_Elevation.svg)

British Columbia's [geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography") is epitomized by the variety and intensity of its physical relief, which has defined patterns of [settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement") and industry since colonization.

British Columbia is bordered to the west by the Pacific Ocean and the American state of [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), to the north by [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon") and the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories"), to the east by the province of [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), and to the south by the American states of [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"), and [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"). The [southern border of British Columbia](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border") was established by the 1846 Oregon Treaty, although its history is tied with lands as far south as California. British Columbia's land area is 944,735 square kilometres (364,764 sq mi). [British Columbia's rugged coastline](/wiki/British_Columbia_Coast "British Columbia Coast") stretches for more than 27,000 kilometres (17,000 mi), and includes deep, mountainous fjords and about 6,000 islands, most of which are uninhabited. It is the only province in Canada that borders the Pacific Ocean. British Columbia's highest mountain is [Mount Fairweather](/wiki/Mount_Fairweather "Mount Fairweather"); the highest mountain entirely within the province is [Mount Waddington](/wiki/Mount_Waddington "Mount Waddington"). British Columbia is considered part of the [Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest") and the [Cascadia bioregion](/wiki/Cascadia_(bioregion) "Cascadia (bioregion)"), along with the [American states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of Alaska, Idaho, (western) Montana, [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), Washington, and [(northern) California](/wiki/Northern_California "Northern California").[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)

British Columbia's capital city, [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia"), is located on the southeastern tip of [Vancouver Island](/wiki/Vancouver_Island "Vancouver Island"). Only a narrow strip of Vancouver Island, from [Campbell River](/wiki/Campbell_River,_British_Columbia "Campbell River, British Columbia") to Victoria, is significantly populated. Much of the western part of Vancouver Island and the rest of the coast is covered by [temperate rainforest](/wiki/Temperate_rainforest "Temperate rainforest"). The province's most populous city, [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), sits at the confluence of the [Fraser River](/wiki/Fraser_River "Fraser River") and [Georgia Strait](/wiki/Georgia_Strait "Georgia Strait") in the southwest corner of the mainland, an area commonly known as the [Lower Mainland](/wiki/Lower_Mainland "Lower Mainland"). By land area, [Abbotsford](/wiki/Abbotsford,_British_Columbia "Abbotsford, British Columbia") is the province's largest city. [Vanderhoof](/wiki/Vanderhoof,_British_Columbia "Vanderhoof, British Columbia") is near the geographic centre of the province.[[32]](#cite_note-34) The northern, mostly mountainous, two-thirds of the province is largely unpopulated and undeveloped, except for the area east of the [Rockies](/wiki/Canadian_Rockies "Canadian Rockies"), where the [Peace River Country](/wiki/Peace_River_Country "Peace River Country") contains BC's portion of the [Canadian Prairies](/wiki/Canadian_Prairies "Canadian Prairies"), centred at the city of [Dawson Creek](/wiki/Dawson_Creek,_British_Columbia "Dawson Creek, British Columbia").

[![Outline map of British Columbia with significant cities and towns.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Bcmap.png/250px-Bcmap.png)](/wiki/File:Bcmap.png)

Outline map of British Columbia with significant cities and towns

The [Coast Mountains](/wiki/Coast_Mountains "Coast Mountains") and the [Inside Passage](/wiki/Inside_Passage "Inside Passage")'s many [inlets](/wiki/Fjord "Fjord") provide some of British Columbia's renowned and spectacular scenery, which forms the backdrop and context for a growing outdoor adventure and [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism") industry. 75 percent of the province is mountainous (more than 1,000 m [3,300 ft] [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level")); 60 percent is forested; and only about 5 percent is arable.

The province's mainland away from the coastal regions is somewhat moderated by the Pacific Ocean. Terrain ranges from dry inland forests and [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") valleys, to the range and canyon districts of the [Central and Southern Interior](/wiki/British_Columbia_Interior "British Columbia Interior"), to boreal forest and subarctic prairie in the Northern Interior. High mountain regions both north and south have [subalpine flora](/wiki/Subalpine_zone "Subalpine zone")[[33]](#cite_note-Subalpine-35) and [subalpine climate](/wiki/Subalpine_climate "Subalpine climate").

The [Okanagan wine area](/wiki/Okanagan_Valley_(wine_region) "Okanagan Valley (wine region)"), extending from [Vernon](/wiki/Vernon,_British_Columbia "Vernon, British Columbia") to [Osoyoos](/wiki/Osoyoos "Osoyoos") at the [Oroville–Osoyoos Border Crossing](/wiki/Oroville%E2%80%93Osoyoos_Border_Crossing "Oroville–Osoyoos Border Crossing"), is one of several wine and [cider](/wiki/Cider "Cider")-producing regions in Canada. Other wine regions in British Columbia include the [Cowichan Valley](/wiki/Cowichan_Valley "Cowichan Valley") on Vancouver Island and the [Fraser Valley](/wiki/Fraser_Valley "Fraser Valley").

The Southern Interior cities of [Kamloops](/wiki/Kamloops "Kamloops") and [Penticton](/wiki/Penticton "Penticton") have some of the warmest and longest summer climates in Canada (while higher elevations are cold and snowy), although their temperatures are often exceeded north of the [Fraser Canyon](/wiki/Fraser_Canyon "Fraser Canyon"), close to the confluence of the Fraser and [Thompson](/wiki/Thompson_River "Thompson River") rivers, where the terrain is rugged and covered with desert-type flora. Semi-desert grassland is found in large areas of the [Interior Plateau](/wiki/Interior_Plateau "Interior Plateau"), with land uses ranging from ranching at lower altitudes to forestry at higher ones.

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/British_Columbia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-British_Columbia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:British_Columbia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") in British Columbia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Strait_of_Georgia.jpg/250px-Strait_of_Georgia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Strait_of_Georgia.jpg)

The [Strait of Georgia](/wiki/Strait_of_Georgia "Strait of Georgia"), near [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver")

Because of the many mountain ranges and rugged coastline, British Columbia's climate varies dramatically across the province.

Coastal southern British Columbia has a mild and rainy climate influenced by the [North Pacific Current](/wiki/North_Pacific_Current "North Pacific Current"). Most of the region is classified as [oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"), though pockets of [warm-summer Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Warm-summer_Mediterranean "Warm-summer Mediterranean") also exist in the far-southern parts of the coast. Precipitation averages above 1,000 millimetres (39 in) in almost all of the coastal region, and [Hucuktlis Lake](/wiki/Hucuktlis_Lake "Hucuktlis Lake") on [Vancouver Island](/wiki/Vancouver_Island "Vancouver Island") receives an average of 6,903 mm (271.8 in) of rain annually.

Due to the blocking presence of successive mountain ranges, the climate of some of the interior valleys of the province (such as the Thompson, parts of the Fraser Canyon, the southern Cariboo and parts of the Okanagan) is [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") with certain locations receiving less than 250 millimetres (10 in) in annual precipitation. The annual mean temperature in the most populated areas of the province is up to 12 °C (54 °F), the mildest anywhere in Canada.

The valleys of the Southern Interior have short winters with only brief bouts of cold or infrequent heavy snow, while those in the [Cariboo](/wiki/Cariboo "Cariboo"), in the [Central Interior](/wiki/British_Columbia_Interior#Central_Interior "British Columbia Interior"), are colder because of increased altitude and latitude, but without the intensity or duration experienced at similar latitudes elsewhere in Canada. Outside of the driest valleys, the Southern and Central Interior generally have a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") with widely variable precipitation. For example, the average daily low in [Prince George](/wiki/Prince_George,_British_Columbia "Prince George, British Columbia") (roughly in the middle of the province) in January is −12 °C (10 °F).[[34]](#cite_note-36) Small towns in the southern interior with high elevation such as [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton,_British_Columbia "Princeton, British Columbia") are typically colder and snowier than cities in the valleys.[[35]](#cite_note-Princeton-37)

Heavy snowfall occurs in all elevated mountainous terrain providing bases for skiers in both south and central British Columbia. Annual snowfall on highway mountain passes in the southern interior rivals some of the snowiest cities in Canada,[[36]](#cite_note-38) and freezing rain and fog are sometimes present on such roads as well.[[37]](#cite_note-39) This can result in hazardous driving conditions, as people are usually travelling between warmer areas such as Vancouver or Kamloops, and may be unaware that the conditions may be slippery and cold.[[38]](#cite_note-Coq-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Shuswap_Lake_from_Sorrento%2C_B.C..jpg/250px-Shuswap_Lake_from_Sorrento%2C_B.C..jpg)](/wiki/File:Shuswap_Lake_from_Sorrento,_B.C..jpg)

[Shuswap Lake](/wiki/Shuswap_Lake "Shuswap Lake") as seen from [Sorrento](/wiki/Sorrento,_British_Columbia "Sorrento, British Columbia")

Winters are generally severe in the Northern Interior which is generally in the [subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") zone, but even there, milder air can penetrate far inland. The coldest temperature in British Columbia was recorded in [Smith River](/wiki/Smith_River,_British_Columbia "Smith River, British Columbia"), where it dropped to −58.9 °C (−74.0 °F) on January 31, 1947,[[39]](#cite_note-KCH-WD2-2002-41) one of the coldest readings recorded anywhere in North America. [Atlin](/wiki/Atlin,_British_Columbia "Atlin, British Columbia") in the province's far northwest, along with the adjoining Southern Lakes region of [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon"), get midwinter thaws caused by the [Chinook](/wiki/Chinook_(wind) "Chinook (wind)") effect, which is also common (and much warmer) in more southerly parts of the Interior.

During winter on the coast, rainfall, sometimes relentless heavy rain, dominates because of consistent barrages of cyclonic low-pressure systems from the North Pacific. Average snowfall on the coast during a normal winter is between 25 and 50 centimetres (10 and 20 in), but on occasion (and not every winter) heavy snowfalls with more than 20 centimetres (8 in) and well below freezing temperatures arrive when modified arctic air reaches coastal areas, typically for short periods, and can take temperatures below −10 °C (14 °F), even at sea level. Arctic outflow winds can occasionally result in wind chill temperatures at or even below −17.8 °C (0.0 °F).[[40]](#cite_note-42) While winters are very wet, coastal areas are generally milder and dry during summer under the influence of stable anti-cyclonic high pressure.

Southern Interior valleys are hot in summer; for example, in [Osoyoos](/wiki/Osoyoos "Osoyoos"), the July maximum temperature averages 31.7 °C (89.1 °F), making it the hottest month of any location in Canada; this hot weather sometimes spreads towards the coast or to the far north of the province. Temperatures often exceed 40 °C (104 °F) in the lower elevations of valleys in the Interior during mid-summer, with the record high of 49.6 °C (121.3 °F) being held in [Lytton](/wiki/Lytton,_British_Columbia "Lytton, British Columbia") on June 29, 2021, during [a record-breaking heat wave that year](/wiki/2021_Western_North_America_heat_wave "2021 Western North America heat wave").[[41]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Vineyards_Lake_Okanagan.jpg/250px-Vineyards_Lake_Okanagan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vineyards_Lake_Okanagan.jpg)

The [Okanagan](/wiki/Okanagan "Okanagan") region has a climate suitable for vineyards.

The extended summer dryness often creates conditions that spark forest fires, from dry-lightning or man-made causes. Many areas of the province are often covered by a blanket of heavy cloud and low fog during the winter months, in contrast to abundant summer sunshine. Annual sunshine hours vary from 2200 near Cranbrook and Victoria to less than 1300 in [Prince Rupert](/wiki/Prince_Rupert,_British_Columbia "Prince Rupert, British Columbia"), on the [North Coast](/wiki/British_Columbia_Coast#North_Coast "British Columbia Coast") just south of [Southeast Alaska](/wiki/Southeast_Alaska "Southeast Alaska").

The exception to British Columbia's wet and cloudy winters is during the [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño") phase. During El Niño events, the jet stream is much farther south across North America, making the province's winters milder and drier than normal. Winters are much wetter and cooler during the opposite phase, [La Niña](/wiki/La_Ni%C3%B1a "La Niña").

Main articles: [List of protected areas of British Columbia](/wiki/List_of_protected_areas_of_British_Columbia "List of protected areas of British Columbia") and [List of British Columbia Provincial Parks](/wiki/List_of_British_Columbia_Provincial_Parks "List of British Columbia Provincial Parks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Lake_O%27Hara_Yoho_National_Park.jpg/250px-Lake_O%27Hara_Yoho_National_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_O%27Hara_Yoho_National_Park.jpg)

[Odaray Mountain](/wiki/Odaray_Mountain "Odaray Mountain") and [Lake O'Hara](/wiki/Lake_O%27Hara "Lake O'Hara")

There are 14 designations of parks and protected areas in the province that reflect the different administration and creation of these areas in a modern context. There are 141 ecological reserves, 35 provincial marine parks, 7 provincial heritage sites, 6 [National Historic Sites of Canada](/wiki/National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada "National Historic Sites of Canada"), 4 [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park") and 3 national park reserves. 12.5 percent of the province's area (114,000 km2 or 44,000 sq mi) is considered protected under one of the 14 different designations that includes over 800 distinct areas.

British Columbia contains seven of [Canada's national parks](/wiki/National_Parks_of_Canada "National Parks of Canada") and National Park Reserves:

* [Glacier National Park](/wiki/Glacier_National_Park_(Canada) "Glacier National Park (Canada)")
* [Gulf Islands National Park Reserve](/wiki/Gulf_Islands_National_Park_Reserve "Gulf Islands National Park Reserve")
* [Gwaii Haanas National Park Reserve and Haida Heritage Site](/wiki/Gwaii_Haanas_National_Park_Reserve_and_Haida_Heritage_Site "Gwaii Haanas National Park Reserve and Haida Heritage Site")
* [Kootenay National Park](/wiki/Kootenay_National_Park "Kootenay National Park")
* [Mount Revelstoke National Park](/wiki/Mount_Revelstoke_National_Park "Mount Revelstoke National Park")
* [Pacific Rim National Park Reserve](/wiki/Pacific_Rim_National_Park_Reserve "Pacific Rim National Park Reserve")
* [Yoho National Park](/wiki/Yoho_National_Park "Yoho National Park")

British Columbia contains a large number of [provincial parks](/wiki/Provincial_park "Provincial park"), run by BC Parks under the aegis of the Ministry of Environment. British Columbia's provincial parks system is the second largest parks system in Canada, the largest being Canada's National Parks system.

Another tier of parks in British Columbia are [regional parks](/wiki/Regional_park "Regional park"), which are maintained and run by [the province's regional districts](/wiki/List_of_regional_districts_of_British_Columbia "List of regional districts of British Columbia"). The Ministry of Forests operates forest recreation sites.

In addition to these areas, over 47,000 square kilometres (18,000 sq mi) of [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land") are protected by the [Agricultural Land Reserve](/wiki/Agricultural_Land_Reserve "Agricultural Land Reserve").

### Fauna

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Awesome_Takakkaw_Falls_IMG_4771.JPG/250px-Awesome_Takakkaw_Falls_IMG_4771.JPG)](/wiki/File:Awesome_Takakkaw_Falls_IMG_4771.JPG)

[Yoho National Park](/wiki/Yoho_National_Park "Yoho National Park")

Much of the province is undeveloped, so populations of many mammalian species that have become rare in much of the United States still flourish in British Columbia.[[43]](#cite_note-45) Watching animals of various sorts, including a very wide range of birds, has long been popular. Bears ([grizzly](/wiki/Grizzly_bear "Grizzly bear"), [black](/wiki/American_black_bear "American black bear")—including the [Kermode bear](/wiki/Kermode_bear "Kermode bear") or spirit bear) live here, as do [deer](/wiki/Deer "Deer"), [elk](/wiki/Elk "Elk"), [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose"), [caribou](/wiki/Reindeer "Reindeer"), [big-horn sheep](/wiki/Bighorn_sheep "Bighorn sheep"), [mountain goats](/wiki/Mountain_goat "Mountain goat"), [marmots](/wiki/Marmot "Marmot"), [beavers](/wiki/Beaver "Beaver"), [muskrats](/wiki/Muskrat "Muskrat"), [coyotes](/wiki/Coyote "Coyote"), [wolves](/wiki/Wolf "Wolf"), [mustelids](/wiki/Mustelidae "Mustelidae") (such as [wolverines](/wiki/Wolverine "Wolverine"), [badgers](/wiki/Badger "Badger") and [fishers](/wiki/Fisher_(animal) "Fisher (animal)")), [cougars](/wiki/Cougar "Cougar"), [eagles](/wiki/Eagle "Eagle"), [ospreys](/wiki/Osprey "Osprey"), [herons](/wiki/Heron "Heron"), [Canada geese](/wiki/Canada_goose "Canada goose"), [swans](/wiki/Swan "Swan"), [loons](/wiki/Loon "Loon"), [hawks](/wiki/Hawk "Hawk"), owls, [ravens](/wiki/Common_raven "Common raven"), [harlequin ducks](/wiki/Harlequin_duck "Harlequin duck"), and many other sorts of ducks. Smaller birds ([robins](/wiki/American_robin "American robin"), [jays](/wiki/Jay "Jay"), [grosbeaks](/wiki/Grosbeak "Grosbeak"), [chickadees](/wiki/Tit_(bird) "Tit (bird)"), and so on) also abound.[[44]](#cite_note-46) Murrelets are known from Frederick Island, a small island off the coast of [Haida Gwaii](/wiki/Haida_Gwaii "Haida Gwaii").[[45]](#cite_note-47)

Many healthy populations of fish are present, including [salmonids](/wiki/Salmonidae "Salmonidae") such as several species of [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon"), [trout](/wiki/Trout "Trout"), [steelhead](/wiki/Rainbow_trout "Rainbow trout"), and [char](/wiki/Salvelinus "Salvelinus"). Besides salmon and trout, sport-fishers in BC also catch [halibut](/wiki/Halibut "Halibut"), [bass](/wiki/Bass_(fish) "Bass (fish)"), and [sturgeon](/wiki/Sturgeon "Sturgeon"). On the coast, [harbour seals](/wiki/Harbour_seal "Harbour seal") and [river otters](/wiki/North_American_river_otter "North American river otter") are common.[[46]](#cite_note-48) [Cetacean](/wiki/Cetacea "Cetacea") species native to the coast include the [orca](/wiki/Orca "Orca"), [humpback whale](/wiki/Humpback_whale "Humpback whale"), [grey whale](/wiki/Grey_whale "Grey whale"), [harbour porpoise](/wiki/Harbour_porpoise "Harbour porpoise"), [Dall's porpoise](/wiki/Dall%27s_porpoise "Dall's porpoise"), [Pacific white-sided dolphin](/wiki/Pacific_white-sided_dolphin "Pacific white-sided dolphin") and [minke whale](/wiki/Minke_whale "Minke whale").

## History

Main article: [History of British Columbia](/wiki/History_of_British_Columbia "History of British Columbia")

### Indigenous societies

Main articles: [Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Pacific_Northwest_Coast "Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast"), [Indigenous peoples of the Northwest Plateau](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Northwest_Plateau "Indigenous peoples of the Northwest Plateau"), and [The Canadian Crown and Aboriginal peoples](/wiki/The_Canadian_Crown_and_Aboriginal_peoples "The Canadian Crown and Aboriginal peoples")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Namgis_%28Native_American%29._Thunderbird_Transformation_Mask%2C_19th_century.jpg/250px-Namgis_%28Native_American%29._Thunderbird_Transformation_Mask%2C_19th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Namgis_(Native_American)._Thunderbird_Transformation_Mask,_19th_century.jpg)

[ʼNamgis](/wiki/%CA%BCNamgis "ʼNamgis") *Thunderbird Transformation Mask*, 19th century

The area now known as British Columbia is home to First Nations groups with several indigenous languages. There are more than 200 First Nations in BC. Prior to contact (with non-Aboriginal people), human history is known from oral histories, archaeological investigations, and from early records from explorers encountering societies early in the period.

The arrival of [Paleoindians](/wiki/Paleoindians "Paleoindians") from [Beringia](/wiki/Beringia "Beringia") took place between 20,000 and 12,000 years ago.[[50]](#cite_note-Muckle2007-52) [Hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") families were the main social structure from 10,000 to 5,000 years ago.[[51]](#cite_note-BastianMitchell2004-53) The nomadic population lived in non-permanent structures foraging for nuts, berries and edible roots while hunting and trapping larger and small game for food and furs.[[51]](#cite_note-BastianMitchell2004-53) Around 5,000 years ago individual groups started to focus on resources available to them locally. Coast Salish peoples had complex land management practices linked to ecosystem health and resilience. Forest gardens on Canada's northwest coast included crabapple, hazelnut, cranberry, wild plum, and wild cherry species.[[52]](#cite_note-54) Thus with the passage of time there is a pattern of increasing regional generalization with a more [sedentary lifestyle](/wiki/Sedentary_lifestyle "Sedentary lifestyle").[[51]](#cite_note-BastianMitchell2004-53) These indigenous populations evolved over the next 5,000 years across a large area into many groups with shared traditions and customs.

To the northwest of the province are the peoples of the [Na-Dene languages](/wiki/Na-Dene_languages "Na-Dene languages"), which include the [Athapaskan-speaking peoples](/wiki/Athapaskan_languages "Athapaskan languages") and the [Tlingit](/wiki/Tlingit_people "Tlingit people"), who live on the islands of southern Alaska and northern British Columbia. The Na-Dene language group is believed to be linked to the [Yeniseian languages](/wiki/Yeniseian_languages "Yeniseian languages") of Siberia:[[53]](#cite_note-BENGTSON-55) the [Dene](/wiki/Dene "Dene") of the western Arctic may represent a distinct wave of migration from Asia to North America.[[53]](#cite_note-BENGTSON-55) The [Interior of British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia_Interior "British Columbia Interior") is home to the [Salishan language](/wiki/Salishan_languages "Salishan languages") groups such as the [Shuswap (Secwepemc)](/wiki/Secwepemc "Secwepemc"), [Okanagan](/wiki/Okanagan_people "Okanagan people") and Athabaskan language groups, primarily the [Dakelh](/wiki/Dakelh "Dakelh") (Carrier) and the [Tsilhqotʼin](/wiki/Chilcotin_people "Chilcotin people").[[54]](#cite_note-Archives-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Kwakiutl_house_pole_InvMH975-123-1.jpg/250px-Kwakiutl_house_pole_InvMH975-123-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kwakiutl_house_pole_InvMH975-123-1.jpg)

[Kwakwaka'wakw](/wiki/Kwakwaka%27wakw "Kwakwaka'wakw") house pole depicting a woman holding an infant, second half of the 19th century

The inlets and valleys of the British Columbia coast shelter large, distinctive populations, such as the [Haida](/wiki/Haida_people "Haida people"), [Kwakwakaʼwakw](/wiki/Kwakwaka%CA%BCwakw "Kwakwakaʼwakw") and [Nuu-chah-nulth](/wiki/Nuu-chah-nulth_people "Nuu-chah-nulth people"), sustained by the region's abundant salmon and shellfish.[[54]](#cite_note-Archives-56) These peoples developed [complex cultures](/wiki/Complex_society "Complex society") dependent on the [western red cedar](/wiki/Western_red_cedar "Western red cedar") that included wooden houses, seagoing whaling and war canoes and elaborately carved [potlatch](/wiki/Potlatch "Potlatch") items and [totem poles](/wiki/Totem_poles "Totem poles").[[54]](#cite_note-Archives-56)

Contact with Europeans brought a series of devastating epidemics of diseases the people had no immunity to.[[55]](#cite_note-57) The population dramatically collapsed, culminating in the 1862 smallpox outbreak in Victoria that spread throughout the coast. European settlement did not bode well for the remaining native population of British Columbia. Colonial officials deemed colonists could make better use of the land than the First Nations people, and thus the land should be owned by the colonists.[[56]](#cite_note-gilmartin2009-58): 120 To ensure colonists would be able to settle properly and make use of the land, First Nations were forcibly relocated onto [reserves](/wiki/Indian_reserve "Indian reserve"), which were often too small to support their way of life.[[56]](#cite_note-gilmartin2009-58): 120–121 By the 1930s, British Columbia had over 1500 reserves.[[56]](#cite_note-gilmartin2009-58): 121

### Fur trade and colonial era

Main article: [Columbia District](/wiki/Columbia_District "Columbia District")

See also: [Oregon Country](/wiki/Oregon_Country "Oregon Country") and [Oregon boundary dispute](/wiki/Oregon_boundary_dispute "Oregon boundary dispute")

Lands now known as British Columbia were added to the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") during the 19th century. Colonies originally begun with the support of the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") (Vancouver Island, the mainland) were amalgamated, then entered Confederation as British Columbia in 1871 as part of the Dominion of Canada.

During the 1770s, [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") killed at least 30 percent of the Pacific Northwest [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada").[[57]](#cite_note-59) This devastating epidemic was the first in a series; the [1862 Pacific Northwest smallpox epidemic](/wiki/1862_Pacific_Northwest_smallpox_epidemic "1862 Pacific Northwest smallpox epidemic") killed about half to two-thirds of the native population of what became British Columbia.[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-Boyd-61)[[60]](#cite_note-lange-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Fort_Victoria_watercolour.jpg/250px-Fort_Victoria_watercolour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_Victoria_watercolour.jpg)

Fort Victoria in 1845

The arrival of Europeans began around the mid-18th century, as [fur traders](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") entered the area to harvest [sea otters](/wiki/Sea_otter "Sea otter"). While it is thought [Francis Drake](/wiki/Francis_Drake "Francis Drake") may have explored the British Columbian coast in 1579, it was [Juan Pérez](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_P%C3%A9rez_Hern%C3%A1ndez "Juan José Pérez Hernández") who completed the first documented voyage, which took place in 1774. [Juan Francisco de la Bodega y Quadra](/wiki/Juan_Francisco_de_la_Bodega_y_Quadra "Juan Francisco de la Bodega y Quadra") explored the coast in 1775. In doing so, Pérez and Quadra reasserted the [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") claim for the [Pacific coast](/wiki/Pacific_coast "Pacific coast"), first made by [Vasco Núñez de Balboa](/wiki/Vasco_N%C3%BA%C3%B1ez_de_Balboa "Vasco Núñez de Balboa") in 1513.

The explorations of [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") in 1778 and [George Vancouver](/wiki/George_Vancouver "George Vancouver") in 1792 and 1793 established British jurisdiction over the coastal area north and west of the Columbia River. In 1793, [Alexander Mackenzie](/wiki/Alexander_Mackenzie_(explorer) "Alexander Mackenzie (explorer)") was the first European to journey across North America overland to the Pacific Ocean, inscribing a stone marking his accomplishment on the shoreline of [Dean Channel](/wiki/Dean_Channel "Dean Channel") near [Bella Coola](/wiki/Bella_Coola,_British_Columbia "Bella Coola, British Columbia"). His expedition theoretically established British sovereignty inland, and a succession of other fur company explorers charted the maze of rivers and mountain ranges between the Canadian Prairies and the Pacific. Mackenzie and other explorers—notably [John Finlay](/wiki/John_Finlay_(fur_trader) "John Finlay (fur trader)"), [Simon Fraser](/wiki/Simon_Fraser_(explorer) "Simon Fraser (explorer)"), [Samuel Black](/wiki/Samuel_Black "Samuel Black"), and [David Thompson](/wiki/David_Thompson_(explorer) "David Thompson (explorer)")—were primarily concerned with extending the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade"), rather than political considerations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1794, by the third of a series of agreements known as the [Nootka Conventions](/wiki/Nootka_Convention "Nootka Convention"), [Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") conceded its claims of exclusivity in the Pacific. This opened the way for formal claims and colonization by other powers, including Britain, but because of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), there was little British action on its claims in the region until later.

The establishment of [trading posts](/wiki/Trading_post "Trading post") by the [North West Company](/wiki/North_West_Company "North West Company") and the Hudson's Bay Company (HBC), effectively established a permanent British presence in the region. The Columbia District was broadly defined as being south of 54°40′ north latitude, (the southern limit of [Russian America](/wiki/Russian_colonization_of_the_Americas "Russian colonization of the Americas")), north of Mexican-controlled California, and west of the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains"). It was, by the [Anglo-American Convention of 1818](/wiki/Treaty_of_1818 "Treaty of 1818"), under the "joint occupancy and use" of citizens of the United States and subjects of Britain.[[61]](#cite_note-63) This co-occupancy was ended with the [Oregon Treaty](/wiki/Oregon_Treaty "Oregon Treaty") of 1846.

The major supply route was the [York Factory Express](/wiki/York_Factory_Express "York Factory Express") between [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay") and [Fort Vancouver](/wiki/Fort_Vancouver "Fort Vancouver"). Some of the early outposts grew into settlements, communities and cities. Among the places in British Columbia that began as fur trading posts are [Fort St. John](/wiki/Fort_St._John,_British_Columbia "Fort St. John, British Columbia") (established 1794); [Hudson's Hope](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Hope "Hudson's Hope") (1805); [Fort Nelson](/wiki/Fort_Nelson,_British_Columbia "Fort Nelson, British Columbia") (1805); [Fort St. James](/wiki/Fort_St._James "Fort St. James") (1806); [Prince George](/wiki/Prince_George,_British_Columbia "Prince George, British Columbia") (1807); [Kamloops](/wiki/Kamloops "Kamloops") (1812); [Fort Langley](/wiki/Fort_Langley "Fort Langley") (1827); [Fort Victoria](/wiki/Fort_Victoria_(British_Columbia) "Fort Victoria (British Columbia)") (1843); [Yale](/wiki/Yale,_British_Columbia "Yale, British Columbia") (1848); and [Nanaimo](/wiki/Nanaimo "Nanaimo") (1853). Fur company posts that became cities in what is now the United States include [Vancouver, Washington](/wiki/Vancouver,_Washington "Vancouver, Washington") (Fort Vancouver), formerly the "capital" of [Hudson's Bay](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") operations in the Columbia District, [Colville](/wiki/Colville,_Washington "Colville, Washington") and [Walla Walla](/wiki/Walla_Walla,_Washington "Walla Walla, Washington") (old [Fort Nez Percés](/wiki/Fort_Nez_Perc%C3%A9s "Fort Nez Percés")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Edward_Gennys_Fanshawe%2C_Fort_Rupert%2C_Beaver_Harbour%2C_Vancouver%27s_Island%2C_July_23rd_1851_%28Canada%29.jpg/250px-Edward_Gennys_Fanshawe%2C_Fort_Rupert%2C_Beaver_Harbour%2C_Vancouver%27s_Island%2C_July_23rd_1851_%28Canada%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_Gennys_Fanshawe,_Fort_Rupert,_Beaver_Harbour,_Vancouver%27s_Island,_July_23rd_1851_(Canada).jpg)

[Fort Rupert](/wiki/Fort_Rupert "Fort Rupert"), Vancouver Island, 1851

With the amalgamation of the two fur trading companies in 1821, modern-day British Columbia existed in three fur-trading departments. The bulk of the central and northern interior was organized into the [New Caledonia](/wiki/New_Caledonia_(Canada) "New Caledonia (Canada)") district, administered from [Fort St. James](/wiki/Fort_St._James "Fort St. James"). The interior south of the [Thompson River](/wiki/Thompson_River "Thompson River") watershed and north of the Columbia was organized into the Columbia District, administered from Fort Vancouver on the lower Columbia River. The northeast corner of the province east of the Rockies, known as the Peace River Block, was attached to the much larger [Athabasca District](/wiki/District_of_Athabasca "District of Athabasca"), headquartered in [Fort Chipewyan](/wiki/Fort_Chipewyan "Fort Chipewyan"), in present-day Alberta.

Until 1849, these districts were a wholly unorganized area of [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") under the de facto jurisdiction of HBC administrators; however, unlike [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land") to the north and east, the territory was not a concession to the company. Rather, it was simply granted a monopoly to trade with the First Nations inhabitants. All that was changed with the westward extension of American exploration and the concomitant overlapping claims of territorial sovereignty, especially in the southern [Columbia Basin](/wiki/Columbia_River_drainage_basin "Columbia River drainage basin") (within present day Washington and [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon")). In 1846, the [Oregon Treaty](/wiki/Oregon_Treaty "Oregon Treaty") divided the territory along the [49th parallel](/wiki/49th_parallel_north "49th parallel north") to the [Strait of Georgia](/wiki/Strait_of_Georgia "Strait of Georgia"), with the area south of this boundary (excluding Vancouver Island and the [Gulf Islands](/wiki/Gulf_Islands "Gulf Islands")) transferred to sole American sovereignty. The [Colony of Vancouver Island](/wiki/Colony_of_Vancouver_Island "Colony of Vancouver Island") was created in 1849, with Victoria designated as the capital. New Caledonia, as the whole of the mainland rather than just its north-central Interior came to be called, continued to be an unorganized territory of British North America, "administered" by individual HBC trading post managers.

### Colony of British Columbia (1858–1866)

Main articles: [Colony of British Columbia (1858–66)](/wiki/Colony_of_British_Columbia_(1858%E2%80%9366) "Colony of British Columbia (1858–66)"), [Richard Clement Moody](/wiki/Richard_Clement_Moody "Richard Clement Moody"), and [James Douglas (governor)](/wiki/James_Douglas_(governor) "James Douglas (governor)")

With the [Fraser Canyon Gold Rush](/wiki/Fraser_Canyon_Gold_Rush "Fraser Canyon Gold Rush") in 1858, an influx of Americans into New Caledonia prompted the [colonial office](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_the_Colonies "Secretary of State for the Colonies") to designate the mainland as the Colony of British Columbia. When news of the Fraser Canyon Gold Rush reached London, Richard Clement Moody was hand-picked by the [Colonial Office](/wiki/Colonial_Office "Colonial Office"), under [Edward Bulwer-Lytton](/wiki/Edward_Bulwer-Lytton "Edward Bulwer-Lytton"), to establish British order and to transform the newly established Colony of British Columbia into the British Empire's "bulwark in the farthest west"[[62]](#cite_note-Donald_J._Hauka_2003,_p.146-64) and "found a second England on the shores of the Pacific".[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman200771-65) Lytton desired to send to the colony "representatives of the best of British culture, not just a police force": he sought men who possessed "courtesy, high breeding and urbane knowledge of the world"[[64]](#cite_note-scott1983-66): 13 and he decided to send Moody, whom the government considered to be the archetypal "[English gentleman](/wiki/Landed_gentry "Landed gentry") and British Officer"[[64]](#cite_note-scott1983-66): 19 to lead the [Royal Engineers, Columbia Detachment](/wiki/Royal_Engineers,_Columbia_Detachment "Royal Engineers, Columbia Detachment").

Moody and his family arrived in British Columbia in December 1858, commanding the Royal Engineers, Columbia Detachment.[[10]](#cite_note-Richard_Clement_Moody_Obituary,_ICE-12) He was sworn in as the first [lieutenant governor of British Columbia](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_of_British_Columbia "Lieutenant governor of British Columbia") and appointed Chief Commissioner of Lands and Works for British Columbia. On the advice of Lytton, Moody hired [Robert Burnaby](/wiki/Robert_Burnaby "Robert Burnaby") as his personal secretary.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Cuyp%2C_Aelbert_-_Cattle_near_the_Maas%2C_with_Dordrecht_in_the_distance_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Cuyp%2C_Aelbert_-_Cattle_near_the_Maas%2C_with_Dordrecht_in_the_distance_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cuyp,_Aelbert_-_Cattle_near_the_Maas,_with_Dordrecht_in_the_distance_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Cattle near the Maas* by Dutch painter [Aelbert Cuyp](/wiki/Aelbert_Cuyp "Aelbert Cuyp"). Moody likened his vision of the nascent Colony of British Columbia to the pastoral scenes painted by Cuyp.

In British Columbia, Moody "wanted to build a city of beauty in the wilderness" and planned his city as an iconic visual metaphor for British dominance, "styled and located with the objective of reinforcing the authority of the crown and of the robe".[[64]](#cite_note-scott1983-66): 26 Subsequent to the enactment of the *Pre-emption Act* of 1860, Moody settled the [Lower Mainland](/wiki/Lower_Mainland "Lower Mainland"). He selected the site and founded the new capital, [New Westminster](/wiki/New_Westminster "New Westminster"). He selected the site due to the strategic excellence of its position and the quality of its port.[[64]](#cite_note-scott1983-66): 26 He was also struck by the majestic beauty of the site, writing in his letter to Blackwood,

> The entrance to the Frazer is very striking—Extending miles to the right & left are low marsh lands (apparently of very rich qualities) & yet [fr](/wiki/Sic "Sic") the Background of Superb Mountains-- Swiss in outline, dark in woods, grandly towering into the clouds there is a sublimity that deeply impresses you. Everything is large and magnificent, worthy of the entrance to the Queen of England's dominions on the Pacific mainland. ... My imagination converted the silent marshes into [Cuyp](/wiki/Cuyp "Cuyp")-like pictures of horses and cattle lazily fattening in rich meadows in a glowing sunset. ... The water of the deep clear Frazer was of a glassy stillness, not a ripple before us, except when a fish rose to the surface or broods of wild ducks fluttered away.[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman20077-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Victoria%2C_British_Columbia%2C_1864.jpg/250px-Victoria%2C_British_Columbia%2C_1864.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria,_British_Columbia,_1864.jpg)

Victoria, 1864

Lord Lytton "forgot the practicalities of paying for clearing and developing the site and the town" and the efforts of Moody's engineers were continuously hampered by insufficient funds, which, together with the continuous opposition of [Governor James Douglas](/wiki/James_Douglas_(governor) "James Douglas (governor)"), whom [Thomas Frederick Elliot](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Frederick_Elliot&action=edit&redlink=1 "Thomas Frederick Elliot (page does not exist)") described as "like any other fraud",[[67]](#cite_note-69) "made it impossible for [Moody's] design to be fulfilled".[[68]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-70)[[69]](#cite_note-heraldicscienceheraldique.com-71)[[64]](#cite_note-scott1983-66): 27

Moody and the Royal Engineers also built an extensive road network, including what would become [Kingsway](/wiki/Kingsway_(Vancouver) "Kingsway (Vancouver)"), connecting New Westminster to [False Creek](/wiki/False_Creek "False Creek"), the North Road between [Port Moody](/wiki/Port_Moody "Port Moody") and New Westminster, and the [Cariboo Road](/wiki/Cariboo_Road "Cariboo Road") and [Stanley Park](/wiki/Stanley_Park "Stanley Park").[[70]](#cite_note-royalengineers.ca-72) He named [Burnaby Lake](/wiki/Burnaby_Lake "Burnaby Lake") after his private secretary [Robert Burnaby](/wiki/Robert_Burnaby "Robert Burnaby") and named Port Coquitlam's 400-foot "Mary Hill" after his wife. As part of the surveying effort, several tracts were designated "government reserves", which included Stanley Park as a military reserve (a strategic location in case of an American invasion). The *Pre-emption Act* did not specify conditions for distributing the land, so large parcels were snapped up by speculators, including 1,518 hectares (3,750 acres) by Moody himself. For this he was criticized by local newspapermen for [land grabbing](/wiki/Land_grabbing "Land grabbing"). Moody designed the first [coat of arms of British Columbia](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_British_Columbia "Coat of arms of British Columbia"). Port Moody is named after him. It was established at the end of a trail that connected New Westminster with Burrard Inlet to defend New Westminster from potential attack from the US.

By 1862, the [Cariboo Gold Rush](/wiki/Cariboo_Gold_Rush "Cariboo Gold Rush"), attracting an additional 5000 miners, was underway, and Douglas hastened construction of the Great North Road (commonly known now as the [Cariboo Wagon Road](/wiki/Cariboo_Wagon_Road "Cariboo Wagon Road")) up the [Fraser Canyon](/wiki/Fraser_Canyon "Fraser Canyon") to the prospecting region around [Barkerville](/wiki/Barkerville "Barkerville"). By the time of this gold rush, the character of the colony was changing, as a more stable population of British colonists settled in the region, establishing businesses, opening [sawmills](/wiki/Sawmill "Sawmill"), and engaging in [fishing](/wiki/Fishery "Fishery") and agriculture. With this increased stability, objections to the colony's absentee governor and the lack of [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") began to be vocalized, led by the influential editor of the [New Westminster](/wiki/New_Westminster "New Westminster") *British Columbian* and future [premier](/wiki/Premier_of_British_Columbia "Premier of British Columbia"), [John Robson](/wiki/John_Robson_(politician) "John Robson (politician)"). A series of petitions requesting an assembly were ignored by Douglas and the colonial office until Douglas was eased out of office in 1864. Finally, the colony would have both an assembly and a resident governor.

### Later gold rushes

A series of gold rushes in various parts of the province followed, the largest being the [Cariboo Gold Rush](/wiki/Cariboo_Gold_Rush "Cariboo Gold Rush") in 1862, forcing the colonial administration into deeper debt as it struggled to meet the extensive infrastructure needs of far-flung boom communities like [Barkerville](/wiki/Barkerville,_British_Columbia "Barkerville, British Columbia") and [Lillooet](/wiki/Lillooet "Lillooet"), which sprang up overnight. The Vancouver Island colony was facing financial crises of its own, and pressure to merge the two eventually [succeeded in 1866](/wiki/Colony_of_British_Columbia_(1866%E2%80%9371) "Colony of British Columbia (1866–71)"), when the colony of British Columbia was amalgamated with the [Colony of Vancouver Island](/wiki/Colony_of_Vancouver_Island "Colony of Vancouver Island") to form the [Colony of British Columbia (1866–1871)](/wiki/Colony_of_British_Columbia_(1866%E2%80%931871) "Colony of British Columbia (1866–1871)"), which was, in turn, succeeded by the present day province of British Columbia following the [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") of 1871.

### Rapid growth and development (1860s to 1910s)

Main articles: [Amor De Cosmos](/wiki/Amor_De_Cosmos "Amor De Cosmos"), [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation"), and [Canada in World War I](/wiki/Canada_in_World_War_I "Canada in World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/LastSpike_Craigellachie_BC_Canada.jpg/250px-LastSpike_Craigellachie_BC_Canada.jpg)](/wiki/File:LastSpike_Craigellachie_BC_Canada.jpg)

[Lord Strathcona](/wiki/Donald_Smith,_1st_Baron_Strathcona_and_Mount_Royal "Donald Smith, 1st Baron Strathcona and Mount Royal") drives the [Last Spike](/wiki/Last_Spike_(Canadian_Pacific_Railway) "Last Spike (Canadian Pacific Railway)") of the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway"), at [Craigellachie](/wiki/Craigellachie,_British_Columbia "Craigellachie, British Columbia"), November 7, 1885. Completion of the [transcontinental railroad](/wiki/Transcontinental_railroad "Transcontinental railroad") was a condition of British Columbia's entry into [Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Craigellachie_%28Gedenkstein%29.jpg/250px-Craigellachie_%28Gedenkstein%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Craigellachie_(Gedenkstein).jpg)

Memorial to the "last spike" in Craigellachie

The Confederation League led the chorus pressing for the colony to join Canada, which had been created out of three British North American colonies in 1867 (the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"), Nova Scotia and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick")). With the agreement by the Canadian government to extend the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway") to British Columbia and assume the colony's debt, British Columbia became the sixth province to join [Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") on July 20, 1871. The Treaty of Washington sent the [Pig War](/wiki/Pig_War_(1859) "Pig War (1859)") San Juan Islands Border dispute to arbitration in 1871 and in 1903, the province's territory shrank again after the [Alaska boundary dispute](/wiki/Alaska_boundary_dispute "Alaska boundary dispute") settled the vague boundary of the [Alaska Panhandle](/wiki/Alaska_Panhandle "Alaska Panhandle").

Population in British Columbia continued to expand as the mining, [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry"), agriculture, and [fishing](/wiki/Fishery "Fishery") sectors were developed. Mining activity was notable throughout the Mainland, so a common epithet for it, even after provincehood, was "the Gold Colony".[[71]](#cite_note-73) Agriculture attracted settlers to the fertile Fraser Valley. Cattle ranchers and later fruit growers came to the drier grasslands of the Thompson Rivers, the Cariboo, the [Chilcotin](/wiki/Chilcotin_Country "Chilcotin Country"), and the Okanagan. Forestry drew workers to the temperate rainforests of the coast, which was also the locus of a growing fishery.

The completion of the railway in 1885 contributed to the economy, facilitating the transportation of the region's considerable resources to the east. The milltown of Granville, also known as [Gastown](/wiki/Gastown "Gastown") was selected as the terminus. This prompted the incorporation of the city of Vancouver in 1886. The completion of the [Port of Vancouver](/wiki/Port_of_Vancouver "Port of Vancouver") spurred rapid growth, and in less than fifty years the city surpassed [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg"), Manitoba, as the largest in Western Canada. The early decades of the province were ones in which issues of land use—specifically, its settlement and development—were paramount. This included expropriation from First Nations people of their land, control over its resources, as well as the ability to trade in some resources, such as fishing.

Establishing a [labour force](/wiki/Labor_force "Labor force") to develop the province was problematic, and British Columbia was a destination of immigration from Europe, China, Japan and India. The influx of a non-[European](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") population stimulated resentment from the dominant ethnic groups, resulting in agitation and an attempt to restrict the ability of [Asian people](/wiki/Asian_people "Asian people") to immigrate to British Columbia through the imposition of the [Chinese head tax](/wiki/Chinese_head_tax "Chinese head tax").[[72]](#cite_note-74) This resentment culminated in mob attacks against Chinese and Japanese immigrants in Vancouver in 1887 and 1907.

### 20th century

Further information: [Canada in World War II](/wiki/Canada_in_World_War_II "Canada in World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/British_Columbia_Regiment_1940.jpg/250px-British_Columbia_Regiment_1940.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Columbia_Regiment_1940.jpg)

*[Wait for Me, Daddy](/wiki/Wait_for_Me,_Daddy "Wait for Me, Daddy")*, a photo taken by [Claude P. Dettloff](/wiki/Claude_P._Dettloff "Claude P. Dettloff") of the [British Columbia Regiment](/wiki/The_British_Columbia_Regiment_(Duke_of_Connaught%27s_Own)_(RCAC) "The British Columbia Regiment (Duke of Connaught's Own) (RCAC)") marching in [New Westminster](/wiki/New_Westminster "New Westminster"), October 1940

In [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the province responded strongly to the call to assist the British Empire against its German foes in French and Belgian battlefields. About 55,570 of the province's 400,000 residents, the highest per-capita rate in Canada, responded to the military's need. About 6,225 men from the province died in combat.[[73]](#cite_note-75)

In 1914, a second transcontinental rail line, the [Grand Trunk Pacific](/wiki/Grand_Trunk_Pacific_Railway "Grand Trunk Pacific Railway"), was completed. This opened up the North Coast and [Bulkley Valley](/wiki/Bulkley_Valley "Bulkley Valley") region to new economic opportunities. What had previously been an almost exclusively fur-trading and subsistence economy soon became an area for forestry, farming, and mining. This sector attracted workers from Asia and Europe, leading to a diverse but conflict-ridden society. The early 20th century saw significant interaction between immigrants, [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada"), and economic forces. There was a rise in the labour movement, marked by strikes and conflicts such as the [1935 docker's strike at Ballantyne Pier](/wiki/Battle_of_Ballantyne_Pier "Battle of Ballantyne Pier") and the [On-to-Ottawa Trek](/wiki/On-to-Ottawa_Trek "On-to-Ottawa Trek"). These events underscored tensions between workers and big business, often mediated by the Communist Party. Racial and ethnic relations were strained, with legislation reflecting the era's racial prejudices, notably against Asian immigrants and First Nations. The early and mid-20th century was marred by incidents like the [Komagata Maru incident](/wiki/Komagata_Maru_incident "Komagata Maru incident"), highlighting anti-Asian sentiment.

The interwar period and World War II introduced significant changes, including [prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition "Prohibition") and its eventual repeal, and the [internment of Japanese Canadians](/wiki/Internment_of_Japanese_Canadians "Internment of Japanese Canadians"). The post-war era saw coalition governments and a booming economy, spearheaded by infrastructure projects and industrial expansion. The [Social Credit Party](/wiki/British_Columbia_Social_Credit_Party "British Columbia Social Credit Party"), under [W.A.C. Bennett](/wiki/W.A.C._Bennett "W.A.C. Bennett"), dominated BC politics, initiating major projects and laying the groundwork for future economic growth. The 1970s and 1980s brought economic challenges and political shifts, culminating in the [Expo 86](/wiki/Expo_86 "Expo 86") world's fair and the end of Social Credit dominance. This period also saw significant social movements, such as [Operation Solidarity](/wiki/Operation_Solidarity "Operation Solidarity"). There was a transition to [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party "New Democratic Party") governance in the 1990s, focusing on environmental conservation and economic struggles. In its second term especially, the NDP government faced political scandals, such as the [fast ferry scandal](/wiki/Fast_ferry_scandal "Fast ferry scandal"), that ultimately contributed to its downfall.

### 21st century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Vancouver_2010_Public_Caldron.jpg/250px-Vancouver_2010_Public_Caldron.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vancouver_2010_Public_Caldron.jpg)

The cauldron of the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") in Vancouver

In the [2001 provincial election](/wiki/2001_British_Columbia_general_election "2001 British Columbia general election"), [Gordon Campbell](/wiki/Gordon_Campbell_(Canadian_politician) "Gordon Campbell (Canadian politician)")'s Liberals defeated the NDP, gaining 77 out of 79 total seats in the provincial legislature. Campbell instituted reforms and removed some of the NDP's policies, along with selling off the previous government's "fast ferries", lowering income taxes, and instituting the controversial long-term lease of [BC Rail](/wiki/BC_Rail "BC Rail") to [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway"). Campbell led his party to victory in the [2005 provincial election](/wiki/2005_British_Columbia_general_election "2005 British Columbia general election") against a substantially strengthened NDP opposition and won a third term in the [2009 provincial election](/wiki/2009_British_Columbia_general_election "2009 British Columbia general election").

The province won a bid to host the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") and [Whistler](/wiki/Whistler,_British_Columbia "Whistler, British Columbia"). In 2003, Vancouver's residents had voted in a referendum accepting the responsibilities of the host city should it win its bid. 64 percent of residents voted in favour of hosting.[[74]](#cite_note-76) After the Olympic joy faded, Campbell's popularity fell. His management style, implementation of the [Harmonized Sales Tax](/wiki/Harmonized_Sales_Tax "Harmonized Sales Tax") (HST) despite election promises not to introduce it, and cancellation of the [BC Rail corruption trial](/wiki/British_Columbia_Legislature_raids "British Columbia Legislature raids")[*[disputed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Disputed statement") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:British_Columbia#Cancellation_of_the_BC_Rail_corruption_trial "Talk:British Columbia")*] led to low approval ratings and loss of caucus support: he resigned in November 2010.[[75]](#cite_note-77) In early 2011, former deputy premier [Christy Clark](/wiki/Christy_Clark "Christy Clark") became leader of the Liberals. Early Clark government actions included raising the minimum wage, creating a new statutory holiday in February called "[Family Day](/wiki/Family_Day_(Canada) "Family Day (Canada)")", and pushing the development of BC's [liquefied natural gas](/wiki/Liquefied_natural_gas "Liquefied natural gas") industry. In the lead-up to the [2013 election](/wiki/2013_British_Columbia_general_election "2013 British Columbia general election"), the Liberals lagged behind the NDP by a double-digit gap in the polls but were able to achieve a surprise victory, winning a majority and making Clark the first woman to lead a party to victory in BC.[[76]](#cite_note-78) Her government went on to balance the budget, implement changes to liquor laws and continue with the question of the proposed [Enbridge Northern Gateway Pipelines](/wiki/Enbridge_Northern_Gateway_Pipelines "Enbridge Northern Gateway Pipelines"). In [the 2017 election](/wiki/2017_British_Columbia_general_election "2017 British Columbia general election"), the NDP formed a minority government with the support of the Green Party through a [confidence and supply](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply") agreement. In July 2017, NDP leader John Horgan was sworn in as a premier. Clark resigned and [Andrew Wilkinson](/wiki/Andrew_Wilkinson "Andrew Wilkinson") became leader of the BC Liberals. In the [2020 British Columbia general election](/wiki/2020_British_Columbia_general_election "2020 British Columbia general election"), the NDP won 57 seats and formed a majority government. Wilkinson resigned as the leader of the BC Liberals.

British Columbia has been significantly affected by demographic changes within Canada and around the world. Vancouver was a major destination for many immigrants from [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") who left the former UK colony prior to [its handover](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") to China. Trends of urbanization mean the [Greater Vancouver](/wiki/Greater_Vancouver "Greater Vancouver") area now includes 51 percent of the province's population, followed by [Greater Victoria](/wiki/Greater_Victoria,_British_Columbia "Greater Victoria, British Columbia") with 8 percent. These two metropolitan regions have dominated the demographics of BC.

By 2018, housing prices in Vancouver were the second-least affordable in the world.[[77]](#cite_note-79) Many experts point to evidence of money-laundering from China as a contributing factor. The high price of residential real estate has led to the implementation of an empty homes tax, a housing speculation and vacancy tax, and a foreign buyers' tax on housing.[[78]](#cite_note-80) The net number of people coming to BC from other provinces in 2016 was almost four times larger than in 2012 and BC was the largest net recipient of [interprovincial migrants in Canada](/wiki/Interprovincial_migration_in_Canada "Interprovincial migration in Canada").[[79]](#cite_note-81) In 2023, British Columbia experienced a net population loss of 8,624; a substantial percentage of which were people who moved to [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta").[[80]](#cite_note-82)

By 2021, the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") had had [a major effect on the province](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_British_Columbia "COVID-19 pandemic in British Columbia"),[[81]](#cite_note-83) with over 2,000 deaths and 250,000 confirmed cases. However, the [COVID-19 vaccine](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccine "COVID-19 vaccine") reduced the spread, with 78 percent of people in BC over the age of five having been fully vaccinated. Also in 2021 but unrelated to COVID-19, the [unmarked gravesites of hundreds of Indigenous children](/wiki/Canadian_Indian_residential_school_gravesites "Canadian Indian residential school gravesites") were discovered at three former [Indian residential schools](/wiki/Canadian_Indian_residential_school_system "Canadian Indian residential school system") ([Kamloops](/wiki/Kamloops_Indian_Residential_School "Kamloops Indian Residential School"), [St. Eugene's Mission](/wiki/Kootenay_Indian_Residential_School "Kootenay Indian Residential School"), [Kuper Island](/wiki/Kuper_Island_Indian_Residential_School "Kuper Island Indian Residential School")).[[82]](#cite_note-84)[[83]](#cite_note-:1-85)

Wildfires burned more than 1,000,000 hectares (2,500,000 acres) of British Columbia in 2017, 2018, 2023, and 2024. Wildfires in 2023 burned nearly 3,000,000 hectares (7,400,000 acres) and incurred a cost of more than a billion dollars.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

### Cultural origins

British Columbia is the most diverse province in Canada; as of 2021, the province had the highest proportion of [visible minorities](/wiki/Visible_minority "Visible minority") in the country. ## Economy

Main article: [Economy of British Columbia](/wiki/Economy_of_British_Columbia "Economy of British Columbia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Canada_Place_with_Downtown_Vancouver%2C_British_Columbia%2C_Canada_Masson.jpg/250px-Canada_Place_with_Downtown_Vancouver%2C_British_Columbia%2C_Canada_Masson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canada_Place_with_Downtown_Vancouver,_British_Columbia,_Canada_Masson.jpg)

[Canada Place](/wiki/Canada_Place "Canada Place") in Downtown Vancouver

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Telus_Garden_Entrance_201807.jpg/250px-Telus_Garden_Entrance_201807.jpg)](/wiki/File:Telus_Garden_Entrance_201807.jpg)

Entrance to [Telus Garden](/wiki/Telus_Garden "Telus Garden")

BC's economy is diverse, with service-producing industries accounting for the largest portion of the province's GDP.[[96]](#cite_note-100) It is the terminus of two transcontinental railways, and the site of 27 major marine cargo and passenger terminals. Though less than 5 percent of its vast 944,735 square kilometres (364,764 sq mi) land is [arable](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land"), the province is agriculturally rich (particularly in the [Fraser](/wiki/Fraser_Valley "Fraser Valley") and [Okanagan](/wiki/Okanagan "Okanagan") valleys), because of milder weather near the coast and in certain sheltered southern valleys. Its climate encourages [outdoor recreation](/wiki/Outdoor_recreation "Outdoor recreation") and tourism, though its economic mainstay has long been [resource extraction](/wiki/Resource_extraction "Resource extraction"), principally logging, farming, and mining. Vancouver, the province's largest city, serves as the headquarters of many western-based natural resource companies. It also benefits from a strong housing market and a per capita income well above the national average. While the coast of British Columbia and some valleys in the south-central part of the province have mild weather, the majority of its land mass experiences a cold-winter-temperate climate similar to the rest of Canada. The [Northern Interior](/wiki/Interior_of_British_Columbia "Interior of British Columbia") region has a [subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") with very cold winters. The climate of Vancouver is by far the mildest winter climate of the major Canadian cities, with nighttime January temperatures averaging above the freezing point.[[97]](#cite_note-101)

British Columbia has a history of being a resource dominated economy, centred on the forestry industry but also with fluctuating importance in mining. Employment in the resource sector has fallen steadily as a percentage of employment, and new jobs are mostly in the construction and retail/service sectors. It now has the highest percentage of service industry jobs in the west, constituting 72 percent of industry (compared to 60 percent Western Canadian average).[[98]](#cite_note-102) The largest section of this employment is in finance, insurance, real estate and corporate management; however, many areas outside of metropolitan areas are still heavily reliant on resource extraction. With its film industry known as [Hollywood North](/wiki/Hollywood_North "Hollywood North"), the Vancouver region is the third-largest feature [film production](/wiki/Filmmaking "Filmmaking") location in North America, after [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") and [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[99]](#cite_note-103)

The economic history of British Columbia is replete with tales of [dramatic upswings and downswings](/wiki/Business_cycle "Business cycle"), and this [boom and bust](/wiki/Boom_and_bust "Boom and bust") pattern has influenced the politics, culture and business climate of the province. Economic activity related to mining in particular has widely fluctuated with changes in commodity prices over time, with documented costs to community health.[[100]](#cite_note-104)

In 2020, British Columbia had the third-largest GDP in Canada, with a GDP of $309 billion and a GDP per capita of $60,090.[[101]](#cite_note-105)[[102]](#cite_note-106) British Columbia's [debt-to-GDP ratio](/wiki/Debt-to-GDP_ratio "Debt-to-GDP ratio") is edging up to 15.0 percent in [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year") 2019–20, and it is expected to reach 16.1 percent by 2021–22.[[103]](#cite_note-107)[[104]](#cite_note-108) British Columbia's economy experienced strong growth in recent years with a total growth rate of 9.6% from 2017 to 2021, a growth rate that was second in the country.[[105]](#cite_note-109)
