Coat of arms' [escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") of the current [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_British_Columbia "Lieutenant Governor of British Columbia")

The [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_British_Columbia "Lieutenant Governor of British Columbia"), [Wendy Lisogar-Cocchia](/wiki/Wendy_Lisogar-Cocchia "Wendy Lisogar-Cocchia"), is the Crown's representative in the province. During the absence of the lieutenant governor, the Governor in Council ([federal Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada")) may appoint an administrator to execute the duties of the office. This is usually the [chief justice of British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia_Court_of_Appeal "British Columbia Court of Appeal").[[106]](#cite_note-110) British Columbia is divided into *[regional districts](/wiki/Regional_district "Regional district")* as a means to better enable municipalities and rural areas to work together at a regional level.

British Columbia has a [93-member elected legislative assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_British_Columbia "Legislative Assembly of British Columbia"), elected by the [plurality voting system](/wiki/Plurality_voting_system "Plurality voting system"), though from 2003 to 2009 there was significant debate about switching to a [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote") system called [BC-STV](/wiki/BC-STV "BC-STV"). The government of the day appoints ministers for various portfolios, what are officially part of the [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_British_Columbia "Executive Council of British Columbia"), of whom the [province's premier](/wiki/Premier_of_British_Columbia "Premier of British Columbia") is chair.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/David_Eby_-_2022_%2852507022370%29_%28cropped%29.png/250px-David_Eby_-_2022_%2852507022370%29_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:David_Eby_-_2022_(52507022370)_(cropped).png)

[David Eby](/wiki/David_Eby "David Eby") is [premier](/wiki/Premier_of_British_Columbia "Premier of British Columbia"), BC's [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government").

The province is currently governed by the [British Columbia New Democratic Party](/wiki/British_Columbia_New_Democratic_Party "British Columbia New Democratic Party") (BC NDP) under [Premier](/wiki/Premier_(Canada) "Premier (Canada)") [David Eby](/wiki/David_Eby "David Eby"). The 2017 provincial election saw the Liberal Party take 43 seats, the NDP take 41, and the [British Columbia Green Party](/wiki/British_Columbia_Green_Party "British Columbia Green Party") take 3. No party met the minimum of 44 seats for a majority, therefore leading to the first minority government since 1953. Following the election, the Greens entered into negotiations with both the Liberals and NDP, eventually announcing they would support an NDP minority government. Previously, the right-of-centre [British Columbia Liberal Party](/wiki/British_Columbia_Liberal_Party "British Columbia Liberal Party") governed the province for 16 years between 2001 and 2017, and won the largest landslide election in British Columbia history in 2001, with 77 of 79 seats. The legislature became more evenly divided between the Liberals and NDP following the 2005 (46 Liberal seats of 79) and 2009 (49 Liberal seats of 85) provincial elections. The NDP and its predecessor the [Co-operative Commonwealth Federation](/wiki/Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation "Co-operative Commonwealth Federation") (CCF) have been the main opposition force to right-wing parties since the 1930s and have governed with majority governments in 1972–1975, 1991–2001 and since 2020 (with a minority government from 2017 to 2020). The [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Canada "Green Party of Canada") plays a larger role in the politics of British Columbia than Green parties do in most other jurisdictions in Canada. After a breakthrough election in 2001 (12.39 percent), the party's vote share declined (2005 – 9.17 percent, 2009 – 8.09 percent, 2013 – 8.13 percent) before increasing again to a record high of 16.84 percent at the 2017 election.

The British Columbia Liberal Party is not related to the federal Liberal Party and does not share the same ideology. Instead, the BC Liberal party is a rather diverse coalition, made up of the remnants of the Social Credit Party, many [federal Liberals](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada"), [federal Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada"), and those who would otherwise support right-of-centre or free enterprise parties. In 2022, [Kevin Falcon](/wiki/Kevin_Falcon "Kevin Falcon") was elected leader of the BC Liberals, promising to rename the party in an effort to distance themselves from their federal counterparts. In 2023, the party rebranded as [BC United](/wiki/BC_United "BC United"). Historically, there have commonly been [third parties](/wiki/Third_party_(politics) "Third party (politics)") present in the legislature (including the Liberals themselves from 1952 to 1975); the [BC Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_British_Columbia "Green Party of British Columbia") is the current third party in British Columbia, with three seats in the legislature.

Prior to the rise of the Liberal Party, British Columbia's main political party was the BC Social Credit Party, which governed the province for 20 years. While sharing some ideology with the subsequent Liberal government, they were more right-wing, although they undertook nationalization of various important monopolies, notably BC Hydro and BC Ferries.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Legislative_Assembly_of_British_Columbia_-_20190510.jpg/250px-Legislative_Assembly_of_British_Columbia_-_20190510.jpg)](/wiki/File:Legislative_Assembly_of_British_Columbia_-_20190510.jpg)

The meeting chamber of the Legislative Assembly

British Columbia is known for having politically active labour [unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") who have traditionally supported the NDP or its predecessor, the CCF.

British Columbia's political history is typified by scandal and a cast of colourful characters, beginning with various colonial-era land scandals and abuses of power by early officials (such as those that led to [McGowan's War](/wiki/McGowan%27s_War "McGowan's War") in 1858–59). Notable scandals in Social Credit years included the [Robert Bonner](/wiki/Robert_Bonner_(politician) "Robert Bonner (politician)") Affair and the [Fantasy Gardens](/wiki/Fantasy_Gardens "Fantasy Gardens") scandal which forced Premier Bill Vander Zalm to resign and ended the Social Credit era. NDP scandals included Bingogate, which brought down NDP Premier Mike Harcourt, and the alleged scandal named Casinogate which drove NDP Premier Glen Clark to resign. A variety of scandals plagued the 2001–2017 Liberal government, including Premier Gordon Campbell's arrest for drunk driving in Maui and the resignation of various cabinet ministers because of conflict-of-interest allegations. A [raid on the Parliament Buildings](/wiki/BC_Legislature_Raids "BC Legislature Raids") on December 28, 2003,[[107]](#cite_note-111) in Victoria, including the Premier's Office, resulted in charges only for ministerial aides, although key cabinet members from the time resigned. Campbell eventually resigned in late 2010 due to opposition to his government's plan to introduce a [Harmonized Sales Tax](/wiki/Harmonized_Sales_Tax "Harmonized Sales Tax") (HST) and was replaced by [Christy Clark](/wiki/Christy_Clark "Christy Clark") as premier in the 2011 BC Liberal leadership election.

British Columbia is underrepresented in the [Senate of Canada](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"), leading Premier Christy Clark to refuse to cooperate with the federal government's reforms for senate appointments to be made based on the recommendations of an advisory board that would use non-partisan criteria. Hours after that plan was unveiled in Ottawa on December 3, 2015, Clark issued a statement that it did "not address what's been wrong with the Senate since the beginning".[[108]](#cite_note-112)

The imbalance in representation in that House is apparent when considering population size. The six senators from BC constitute only one for every 775,000 people vs. one for every 75,000 in Prince Edward Island, which has four senators. Nova Scotia and New Brunswick have much smaller populations than BC, yet each has ten senators according to a Global News summary.[[109]](#cite_note-Baldrey-113) Correcting this imbalance would require a constitutional amendment, but that is unlikely to be supported by the Atlantic provinces.[[109]](#cite_note-Baldrey-113)

## Transportation

Transportation played a huge role in British Columbia's history. The Rocky Mountains and the ranges west of them constituted a significant obstacle to overland travel until the completion of the transcontinental railway in 1885. The Peace River Canyon through the Rocky Mountains was the route the earliest explorers and fur traders used. Fur trade routes were only marginally used for access to British Columbia through the mountains. Travel from the rest of Canada before 1885 meant the difficulty of overland travel via the United States, around [Cape Horn](/wiki/Cape_Horn "Cape Horn") or overseas from Asia. Nearly all travel and freight to and from the region occurred via the Pacific Ocean, primarily through the ports of Victoria and New Westminster.

Until the 1930s, rail was the only means of overland travel to and from the rest of Canada; travellers using motor vehicles needed to journey through the United States. With the construction of the Inter-Provincial Highway in 1932 (now known as the [Crowsnest Pass Highway](/wiki/Crowsnest_Highway "Crowsnest Highway")), and later the [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway"), road transportation evolved into the preferred mode of overland travel to and from the rest of the country.

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=British_Columbia&action=edit), the number of [electric vehicles sold in British Columbia](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_vehicles_in_British_Columbia "Plug-in electric vehicles in British Columbia") (as a percentage of total vehicle sales) was the highest of any Canadian province or US state.[[111]](#cite_note-115)

### Roads and highways

Main article: [List of British Columbia provincial highways](/wiki/List_of_British_Columbia_provincial_highways "List of British Columbia provincial highways")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Alex_Fraser_Bridge_from_below.jpg/250px-Alex_Fraser_Bridge_from_below.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alex_Fraser_Bridge_from_below.jpg)

The [Alex Fraser Bridge](/wiki/Alex_Fraser_Bridge "Alex Fraser Bridge") on [Highway 91](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_91 "British Columbia Highway 91") between Richmond and Delta

Because of its size and rugged, varying topography, British Columbia requires thousands of kilometres of provincial highways to connect its communities. British Columbia's roads systems were notoriously poorly maintained and dangerous until a concentrated program of improvement was initiated in the 1950s and 1960s. There are now [freeways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") in Greater Victoria, the Lower Mainland, and Central Interior of the province. Much of the rest of the province, where traffic volumes are generally low, is accessible by well-maintained generally high-mobility two-lane [arterial highways](/wiki/Arterial_road "Arterial road") with additional passing lanes in mountainous areas and usually only a few stop-controlled intersections outside the main urban areas.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Highway_1%2C_Burnaby.jpg/250px-Highway_1%2C_Burnaby.jpg)](/wiki/File:Highway_1,_Burnaby.jpg)

British Columbia Highway 1 near Brentwood, Burnaby

A couple of busy intercity corridors outside Greater Vancouver feature more heavily signalized limited-mobility arterial highways that are mostly four-lane and often divided by portable median [traffic barriers](/wiki/Traffic_barrier "Traffic barrier"). [Highway 1](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_1 "British Columbia Highway 1") on [Vancouver Island](/wiki/Vancouver_Island "Vancouver Island") and Highway 97 through the [Okanagan Valley](/wiki/Okanagan_Valley "Okanagan Valley") are medium- to high-volume roadways with variable posted speeds that range from 50 km/h (31 mph) to maximums just slightly lower than the principal [grade-separated](/wiki/Grade-separated "Grade-separated") highways. Numerous [traffic lights](/wiki/Traffic_light "Traffic light") operate in place of [interchanges](/wiki/Interchange_(road) "Interchange (road)") on both arterials as long-term cost-cutting measures. Signalization along both these highways is heaviest through urban areas and along inter-urban sections where traffic volumes are similar to and sometimes higher than the freeways, but where funding is not available for upgrades to interchanges or construction of high-mobility alternative routes or bypasses. The building and maintenance of provincial highways is the responsibility of the [British Columbia Ministry of Transportation and Infrastructure](/wiki/Ministry_of_Transportation_and_Infrastructure_(British_Columbia) "Ministry of Transportation and Infrastructure (British Columbia)").[[112]](#cite_note-BC_highways-116)

There are only five major routes to the rest of Canada. From south to north they are: [BC Highway 3](/wiki/British_Columbia_provincial_highway_3 "British Columbia provincial highway 3") through the [Crowsnest Pass](/wiki/Crowsnest_Pass "Crowsnest Pass"), the [Vermilion Pass](/wiki/Vermilion_Pass "Vermilion Pass") (Highway 93 in both [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_93 "British Columbia Highway 93") and [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta_Highway_93 "Alberta Highway 93")), the [Kicking Horse Pass](/wiki/Kicking_Horse_Pass "Kicking Horse Pass"), the latter being used by the Trans-Canada Highway entering Alberta through [Banff National Park](/wiki/Banff_National_Park "Banff National Park"), the [Yellowhead Highway](/wiki/Yellowhead_Highway "Yellowhead Highway") (16) through [Jasper National Park](/wiki/Jasper_National_Park "Jasper National Park"), and [Highway 2](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_2 "British Columbia Highway 2") through [Dawson Creek](/wiki/Dawson_Creek "Dawson Creek"). There are also several highway crossings to the adjoining American states of Washington, Idaho, and Montana. The longest highway is Highway 97, running 2,081 kilometres (1,293 mi) from the British Columbia-Washington border at [Osoyoos](/wiki/Osoyoos,_British_Columbia "Osoyoos, British Columbia") north to [Watson Lake, Yukon](/wiki/Watson_Lake,_Yukon "Watson Lake, Yukon") and which includes the British Columbia portion of the [Alaska Highway](/wiki/Alaska_Highway "Alaska Highway").

### Public transit

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Mark_III_SkyTrain_near_Nanaimo_station.jpg/250px-Mark_III_SkyTrain_near_Nanaimo_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mark_III_SkyTrain_near_Nanaimo_station.jpg)

[SkyTrain](/wiki/SkyTrain_(Vancouver) "SkyTrain (Vancouver)") is the rail rapid transit system that serves Metro Vancouver.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Flyer_trolleybuses_on_the_Granville_Mall_in_2019.jpg/250px-Flyer_trolleybuses_on_the_Granville_Mall_in_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flyer_trolleybuses_on_the_Granville_Mall_in_2019.jpg)

[Trolley buses in Vancouver](/wiki/Trolley_buses_in_Vancouver "Trolley buses in Vancouver")

Prior to 1979, surface [public transit](/wiki/Public_transport "Public transport") in the Vancouver and Victoria metropolitan areas was administered by BC Hydro, the provincially owned electricity utility. Subsequently, the province established [BC Transit](/wiki/BC_Transit "BC Transit") to oversee and operate all municipal transportation systems. In 1998, the Greater Vancouver Transportation Authority, now [TransLink](/wiki/TransLink_(British_Columbia) "TransLink (British Columbia)"), a separate authority for routes within the [Metro Vancouver Regional District](/wiki/Metro_Vancouver_Regional_District "Metro Vancouver Regional District"), was established. Some smaller island communities, such as [Gabriola Island](/wiki/Gabriola_Island "Gabriola Island")[[113]](#cite_note-117) and, formerly, [Pender Island](/wiki/Pender_Island "Pender Island")[[114]](#cite_note-118)[[115]](#cite_note-119) operate routes independent of BC Transit or TransLink. BC Transit has recently expanded to provide intercity routes,[[116]](#cite_note-120) particularly in the Northern region of British Columbia. Other intercity routes were introduced connecting southern communities in preparation of the cancellation of [Greyhound Canada](/wiki/Greyhound_Canada "Greyhound Canada")'s pullout from Western Canada,[[117]](#cite_note-121) though options for intercity bus travel are still extremely limited.

Public transit in British Columbia consists mainly of diesel buses, although Vancouver is also serviced by a fleet of [trolley buses](/wiki/Trolley_bus "Trolley bus"). Several experimental buses are being tested such as [hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle") buses that have both gasoline and electric engines. Additionally, there are CNG-fuelled buses being tested and used in Nanaimo and Kamloops systems.[[118]](#cite_note-122) British Columbia also tested a fleet of Hydrogen-fuelled buses for the Vancouver-Whistler Winter Olympics in 2010.[[119]](#cite_note-123) TransLink operates [SkyTrain](/wiki/SkyTrain_(Vancouver) "SkyTrain (Vancouver)"), an automated [metro](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system serving the cities of Vancouver, Burnaby, New Westminster, Surrey, Richmond, Coquitlam, and Port Moody. In 2009, the Canada Line SkyTrain was completed, linking Vancouver International Airport and the city of Richmond to downtown Vancouver bringing the total to three operating metro lines.

A new extension to Coquitlam and [Port Moody](/wiki/Port_Moody "Port Moody") (the [Evergreen Extension](/wiki/Evergreen_Extension "Evergreen Extension") of the [Millennium Line](/wiki/Millennium_Line "Millennium Line")) was completed in December 2016. Construction of an extension of the Millennium Line westwards through Vancouver to Arbutus Street began in February 2021,[[120]](#cite_note-124) with future plans to extend the line farther west from [Arbutus station](/wiki/Arbutus_station "Arbutus station") to the [University of British Columbia](/wiki/University_of_British_Columbia "University of British Columbia").[[121]](#cite_note-125) Fare gates have been added to all existing stations, though in the past, SkyTrain used a proof of payment honour system. In the capital city of Victoria, BC Transit and the provincial government's infrastructure ministry are working together to create a bus rapid transit from the Westshore communities to downtown Victoria.[[122]](#cite_note-126) In [Kamloops](/wiki/Kamloops "Kamloops"), there is a bus rapid transit GPS trial underway to see how bus rapid transit affects smaller cities, rather than larger ones, like Victoria and Vancouver.[[123]](#cite_note-127)

Rail development expanded greatly in the decades after the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway") was completed, in 1885, and was the chief mode of long-distance surface transportation until the expansion and improvement of the provincial highways system began in the 1950s. Two major routes through the [Yellowhead Pass](/wiki/Yellowhead_Pass "Yellowhead Pass") competed with the Canadian Pacific Railway – the [Grand Trunk Pacific Railway](/wiki/Grand_Trunk_Pacific_Railway "Grand Trunk Pacific Railway"), terminating at Prince Rupert, and the [Canadian Northern Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Northern_Railway "Canadian Northern Railway"), terminating at Vancouver.

The [British Columbia Electric Railway](/wiki/British_Columbia_Electric_Railway "British Columbia Electric Railway") provided rail services in Victoria and Vancouver between the nineteenth century and mid twentieth century.

The [Pacific Great Eastern](/wiki/BC_Rail "BC Rail") line supplemented this service, providing a north–south route between interior resource communities and the coast. The Pacific Great Eastern (later known as British Columbia Railway and now owned by Canadian National Railway) connects Fort St James, Fort Nelson, and [Tumbler Ridge](/wiki/Tumbler_Ridge,_British_Columbia "Tumbler Ridge, British Columbia") with [North Vancouver](/wiki/North_Vancouver_(city) "North Vancouver (city)"). The E&N Railway, rebranded as the [Island Rail Corridor](/wiki/Island_Rail_Corridor "Island Rail Corridor"), formerly served the commercial and passenger train markets of Vancouver Island. Service along the route is now minimal. Vancouver Island was also host to the [last logging railway in North America](/wiki/Englewood_Railway "Englewood Railway") until its closure in 2017.

Current passenger services in British Columbia are limited. [Via Rail](/wiki/Via_Rail "Via Rail") operates 10 long-distance trains per week on two lines.[[124]](#cite_note-128) Local services are limited to two regions, with [TransLink](/wiki/TransLink_(British_Columbia) "TransLink (British Columbia)") providing rapid transit and commuter services in the [Lower Mainland](/wiki/Lower_Mainland "Lower Mainland") and by the [Seton Lake Indian Band](/wiki/Seton_Lake_Indian_Band "Seton Lake Indian Band") South of [Lillooet](/wiki/Lillooet "Lillooet") with the [Kaoham Shuttle](/wiki/Kaoham_Shuttle "Kaoham Shuttle"). [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") runs international passenger service between Vancouver, [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), and intermediate points.[[125]](#cite_note-129)

Several [heritage railways](/wiki/Heritage_railway "Heritage railway") operate within the province, including the [White Pass and Yukon Route](/wiki/White_Pass_and_Yukon_Route "White Pass and Yukon Route") that runs between [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") and the [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon") via British Columbia.

### Water

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Spirit_of_Vancouver_Island.jpg/250px-Spirit_of_Vancouver_Island.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spirit_of_Vancouver_Island.jpg)

*Spirit of Vancouver Island* S-class ferry

[BC Ferries](/wiki/BC_Ferries "BC Ferries") was established as a provincial crown corporation in 1960 to provide passenger and vehicle ferry service between Vancouver Island and the Lower Mainland as a cheaper and more reliable alternative to the service operated by the Canadian Pacific Railway and other private operators. It now operates 25 routes among the [islands of British Columbia](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_British_Columbia "List of islands of British Columbia"), as well as between the islands and the mainland. Ferry service to Washington is offered by the [Washington State Ferries](/wiki/Washington_State_Ferries "Washington State Ferries") (between [Sidney](/wiki/Sidney,_British_Columbia "Sidney, British Columbia") and [Anacortes](/wiki/Anacortes,_Washington "Anacortes, Washington")) and [Black Ball Transport](/wiki/MV_Coho "MV Coho") (between Victoria and [Port Angeles, Washington](/wiki/Port_Angeles,_Washington "Port Angeles, Washington")). Ferry service over inland lakes and rivers is provided by the [Ministry of Transportation and Infrastructure](/wiki/British_Columbia_Ministry_of_Transportation_and_Infrastructure "British Columbia Ministry of Transportation and Infrastructure"). Various other coastal ferries are operated privately.

The [SeaBus](/wiki/SeaBus "SeaBus") ferry connects [Lonsdale Quay](/wiki/Lonsdale_Quay "Lonsdale Quay") in the [City of North Vancouver](/wiki/City_of_North_Vancouver "City of North Vancouver") and [Waterfront station](/wiki/Waterfront_station_(Vancouver) "Waterfront station (Vancouver)") in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver").

Commercial ocean transport is of vital importance. Major ports are at Vancouver, [Roberts Bank](/wiki/Roberts_Bank "Roberts Bank") (near [Tsawwassen](/wiki/Tsawwassen,_British_Columbia "Tsawwassen, British Columbia")), Prince Rupert, and Victoria.[[126]](#cite_note-130)[[127]](#cite_note-131)

Vancouver, Victoria, and Prince Rupert are also major ports of call for [cruise ships](/wiki/Cruise_ship "Cruise ship"). In 2007, a large maritime [container port](/wiki/Containerization "Containerization") was opened in Prince Rupert with an inland sorting port in Prince George.

### Air

Main article: [List of airports in British Columbia](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_British_Columbia "List of airports in British Columbia")

There are over 200 airports throughout British Columbia, the major ones being the [Vancouver International Airport](/wiki/Vancouver_International_Airport "Vancouver International Airport"), the [Victoria International Airport](/wiki/Victoria_International_Airport "Victoria International Airport"), the [Kelowna International Airport](/wiki/Kelowna_International_Airport "Kelowna International Airport"), and the [Abbotsford International Airport](/wiki/Abbotsford_International_Airport "Abbotsford International Airport"), the first three of which each served over 1,000,000 passengers in 2005. As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=British_Columbia&action=edit), Vancouver International Airport is the [2nd busiest airport](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Canada "List of the busiest airports in Canada") in the country and the second biggest International Gateway on the west coast (after Los Angeles) with an estimated 26.4 million travellers passing through in 2019.[[128]](#cite_note-132)

## Arts and culture

See also: [List of museums in British Columbia](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_British_Columbia "List of museums in British Columbia")

### Visual arts

See also: [Northwest Coast art](/wiki/Northwest_Coast_art "Northwest Coast art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Emily_Carr_-_Indian_Church.jpg/250px-Emily_Carr_-_Indian_Church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emily_Carr_-_Indian_Church.jpg)

[*Church at Yuquot Village*](/wiki/The_Indian_Church_(painting) "The Indian Church (painting)") by Emily Carr (1929)

The earliest known visual art produced in the [Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest"), and what would become British Columbia, was by First Nations such as the Coast Salish, Haida, Heiltsuk, and Tsimshian, among others. Such Indigenous work comes particularly in the form of [woodcarving](/wiki/Woodcarving "Woodcarving"), as seen in [totem poles](/wiki/Totem_poles "Totem poles"), [transformation masks](/wiki/Transformation_mask "Transformation mask"), and [canoes](/wiki/Canoes "Canoes"), as well as [textile arts](/wiki/Textile_arts "Textile arts") like [Chilkat weaving](/wiki/Chilkat_weaving "Chilkat weaving") and [button blankets](/wiki/Button_blankets "Button blankets"). Traditional Indigenous art of the Pacific Northwest is typically distinguished by the [formline style](/wiki/Formline_art "Formline art"), which is defined as "continuous, flowing, curvilinear lines that turn, swell and diminish in a prescribed manner. They are used for figure outlines, internal design elements and in abstract compositions."[[129]](#cite_note-133)

[Western styles and forms](/wiki/Western_art "Western art") were introduced to the region through the establishment of British North American settlements in the late 18th century. Notable English-Canadian artists of 19th and early 20th century British Columbia include architect [Francis Rattenbury](/wiki/Francis_Rattenbury "Francis Rattenbury"), designer [James Blomfield](/wiki/James_Blomfield "James Blomfield"), and painter [Emily Carr](/wiki/Emily_Carr "Emily Carr").

Vancouver's [art scene](/wiki/Art_scene "Art scene") was dominated by [lyrical abstraction](/wiki/Lyrical_abstraction "Lyrical abstraction") and [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealist_art "Surrealist art") landscape painting in the mid-20th century through such artists as [B. C. Binning](/wiki/B._C._Binning "B. C. Binning"), [Jack Shadbolt](/wiki/Jack_Shadbolt "Jack Shadbolt"), [Gordon A. Smith](/wiki/Gordon_A._Smith "Gordon A. Smith"), [Takao Tanabe](/wiki/Takao_Tanabe "Takao Tanabe"), [Don Jarvis](/wiki/Don_Jarvis "Don Jarvis"), and [Toni Onley](/wiki/Toni_Onley "Toni Onley"). In the following decades, the city would undergo more artistic diversification with the emergence of [conceptual art](/wiki/Conceptual_art "Conceptual art"), [communication art](/wiki/Communication_design "Communication design"), [video art](/wiki/Video_art "Video art"), and performance art.[[130]](#cite_note-134)

The [Vancouver School](/wiki/Vancouver_School "Vancouver School") of [conceptual photography](/wiki/Conceptual_photography "Conceptual photography") encompasses a cohort of Vancouver-based artists who gained notoriety in the 1980s. This school is generally considered to include artists [Jeff Wall](/wiki/Jeff_Wall "Jeff Wall"), [Ian Wallace](/wiki/Ian_Wallace_(artist) "Ian Wallace (artist)"), [Ken Lum](/wiki/Ken_Lum "Ken Lum"), [Roy Arden](/wiki/Roy_Arden "Roy Arden"), [Stan Douglas](/wiki/Stan_Douglas "Stan Douglas"), and [Rodney Graham](/wiki/Rodney_Graham "Rodney Graham").[[131]](#cite_note-135)

Vancouver maintains roughly 350 works of outdoor public art.[[132]](#cite_note-136) Some notable works include *[A-maze-ing Laughter](/wiki/A-maze-ing_Laughter "A-maze-ing Laughter")*, *[Digital Orca](/wiki/Digital_Orca "Digital Orca")*, *[Girl in a Wetsuit](/wiki/Girl_in_a_Wetsuit "Girl in a Wetsuit")*, *[Angel of Victory](/wiki/Angel_of_Victory "Angel of Victory")*, *[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(sculpture) "The Birds (sculpture)")*, and the [Brockton Point](/wiki/Brockton_Point "Brockton Point") totem poles.

### Performing arts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/VSO_Group_Portrait.jpg/250px-VSO_Group_Portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:VSO_Group_Portrait.jpg)

The Vancouver Symphony Orchestra at the Orpheum concert hall (2019)

British Columbia is home to the [Vancouver Opera](/wiki/Vancouver_Opera "Vancouver Opera"), the [City Opera of Vancouver](/wiki/City_Opera_of_Vancouver "City Opera of Vancouver"), [Ballet BC](/wiki/Ballet_BC "Ballet BC"), [contemporary dance](/wiki/Contemporary_dance "Contemporary dance") companies [Holy Body Tattoo](/wiki/Holy_Body_Tattoo "Holy Body Tattoo"), [Kidd Pivot](/wiki/Kidd_Pivot "Kidd Pivot"), [Mascall Dance Society](/wiki/Jennifer_Mascall "Jennifer Mascall"), and [butoh](/wiki/Butoh "Butoh") dance troupe [Kokoro Dance](/wiki/Kokoro_Dance "Kokoro Dance").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It is also the home province for a plethora of independent [theatre companies](/wiki/Theatre_companies "Theatre companies"), including the [Arts Club Theatre Company](/wiki/Arts_Club_Theatre_Company "Arts Club Theatre Company"), the Shakespearean [Bard on the Beach](/wiki/Bard_on_the_Beach "Bard on the Beach"), and [Theatre Under the Stars](/wiki/Theatre_Under_the_Stars_(Vancouver) "Theatre Under the Stars (Vancouver)"). Performing arts venues include the [Queen Elizabeth Theatre](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_Theatre "Queen Elizabeth Theatre"), the [Orpheum Theatre](/wiki/Orpheum_(Vancouver) "Orpheum (Vancouver)"), and the [Royal Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Theatre_(Victoria,_British_Columbia) "Royal Theatre (Victoria, British Columbia)"), among others.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Music

See also: [Music of Vancouver](/wiki/Music_of_Vancouver "Music of Vancouver") and [List of colleges in British Columbia](/wiki/List_of_colleges_in_British_Columbia "List of colleges in British Columbia")

British Columbia is the third largest music-producing province in Canada and the local music industry generates an estimated yearly revenue of $265 million.[[133]](#cite_note-137) The province is home to the [Vancouver Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Vancouver_Symphony_Orchestra "Vancouver Symphony Orchestra"), the [Okanagan Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Okanagan_Symphony_Orchestra "Okanagan Symphony Orchestra"), the [Vancouver Metropolitan Orchestra](/wiki/Vancouver_Metropolitan_Orchestra "Vancouver Metropolitan Orchestra"), the [Vancouver Youth Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Vancouver_Youth_Symphony_Orchestra "Vancouver Youth Symphony Orchestra"), the [Richmond Delta Youth Orchestra](/wiki/Richmond_Delta_Youth_Orchestra "Richmond Delta Youth Orchestra"), and the [Victoria Symphony](/wiki/Victoria_Symphony "Victoria Symphony"). Some important [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") acts include bands such as [Spirit of the West](/wiki/Spirit_of_the_West "Spirit of the West"), [Theory of a Deadman](/wiki/Theory_of_a_Deadman "Theory of a Deadman"), [Trooper](/wiki/Trooper_(band) "Trooper (band)"), [Gob](/wiki/Gob_(band) "Gob (band)"), and [The New Pornographers](/wiki/The_New_Pornographers "The New Pornographers"), and solo artists such as [Bryan Adams](/wiki/Bryan_Adams "Bryan Adams"), [Carly Rae Jepsen](/wiki/Carly_Rae_Jepsen "Carly Rae Jepsen"), [Mac DeMarco](/wiki/Mac_DeMarco "Mac DeMarco"), [Michael Bublé](/wiki/Michael_Bubl%C3%A9 "Michael Bublé"), [Nelly Furtado](/wiki/Nelly_Furtado "Nelly Furtado"), and [Diana Krall](/wiki/Diana_Krall "Diana Krall"). [Music festivals](/wiki/Music_festivals "Music festivals") in BC have included the [Squamish Valley Music Festival](/wiki/Squamish_Valley_Music_Festival "Squamish Valley Music Festival"), [Shambhala Music Festival](/wiki/Shambhala_Music_Festival "Shambhala Music Festival"), and [Pemberton Music Festival](/wiki/Pemberton_Music_Festival "Pemberton Music Festival").[[134]](#cite_note-138)

### Cuisine

See also: [Canadian cuisine](/wiki/Canadian_cuisine "Canadian cuisine"), [Cuisine of the Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Cuisine_of_the_Pacific_Northwest "Cuisine of the Pacific Northwest"), and [Rocky Mountain cuisine](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_cuisine "Rocky Mountain cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/BC_Roll.jpg/250px-BC_Roll.jpg)](/wiki/File:BC_Roll.jpg)

The BC roll is a kind of sushi containing barbecued salmon and cucumber

British Columbian cuisine is commonly associated with healthy living, [fusion](/wiki/Fusion_cuisine "Fusion cuisine"), fresh local ingredients, and innovation.[[135]](#cite_note-Iconic_Foods_of_British_Columbia-139) It can be divided into two broadly-defined traditions: [cuisine associated with the west coast](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest_cuisine "Pacific Northwest cuisine"), which incorporates a variety of seafood elements, and [cuisine associated with the interior of the province](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_cuisine "Rocky Mountain cuisine"), which embraces local [game meat](/wiki/Game_meat "Game meat"), [farm-to-table](/wiki/Farm-to-table "Farm-to-table") produce, and methods of [curing](/wiki/Curing_(food_preservation) "Curing (food preservation)") and [smoking](/wiki/Smoking_(cooking) "Smoking (cooking)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Seafood is an important staple of the province's local food culture due to its proximity to the Pacific Ocean, as well as the region's numerous rivers and lakes. BC is known for several unique dishes and is a producer of fruit, wine,[[136]](#cite_note-Frommers_r561-140) and cheese.[[137]](#cite_note-2022_o454-141)

Seafoods of British Columbia include [sushi](/wiki/Sushi "Sushi") ([BC roll](/wiki/BC_roll "BC roll"), [dynamite roll](/wiki/Dynamite_roll "Dynamite roll"), [California roll](/wiki/California_roll "California roll")), [dungeness crab](/wiki/Dungeness_crab "Dungeness crab") (boiled, [tacos](/wiki/Tacos "Tacos")), [spot prawns](/wiki/Pandalus_platyceros "Pandalus platyceros"), wild [pacific salmon](/wiki/Pacific_salmon "Pacific salmon") (smoked, candied, [teriyaki](/wiki/Teriyaki "Teriyaki"), [chowder](/wiki/Chowder "Chowder"), [sandwich](/wiki/Sandwich "Sandwich")), and [halibut](/wiki/Halibut "Halibut") ([baked](/wiki/Baked "Baked"), lemon ginger), as well as [delicacies](/wiki/Delicacy "Delicacy") like [white sturgeon](/wiki/White_sturgeon "White sturgeon") caviar and [geoduck](/wiki/Geoduck "Geoduck")[[138]](#cite_note-Must_Try_British_Columbia_Recipes-142)[[135]](#cite_note-Iconic_Foods_of_British_Columbia-139)[[139]](#cite_note-Taste_Atlas-143)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Nanaimo_bar_detail.jpg/250px-Nanaimo_bar_detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nanaimo_bar_detail.jpg)

Nanaimo bars originate from the city of [Nanaimo](/wiki/Nanaimo "Nanaimo") and consist of a crumb and nut base, [custard](/wiki/Custard "Custard") middle, and [ganache](/wiki/Ganache "Ganache") top layer

British Columbia is also home to numerous unique non-seafood culinary staples. Some dishes include [borscht](/wiki/Borscht "Borscht"), [Salt Spring Island](/wiki/Salt_Spring_Island "Salt Spring Island") lamb, [Japadog](/wiki/Japadog "Japadog") street food, and [Butter chicken](/wiki/Butter_chicken "Butter chicken") pizza. Some unique pastries include apple cranberry [cinnamon buns](/wiki/Cinnamon_buns "Cinnamon buns"), [Nanaimo bars](/wiki/Nanaimo_bars "Nanaimo bars"), and Victoria creams.[[135]](#cite_note-Iconic_Foods_of_British_Columbia-139)[[138]](#cite_note-Must_Try_British_Columbia_Recipes-142)[[140]](#cite_note-British_Columbia_Food_and_Drink-144) British Columbia also produces several distinct local cheeses, such as kabritt, Castle Blue, and Comox Brie.[[139]](#cite_note-Taste_Atlas-143) The [London Fog](/wiki/London_fog_(beverage) "London fog (beverage)") tea [latte](/wiki/Latte "Latte") was invented in Vancouver and remains a popular beverage among coffee shops in the Pacific Northwest and Western Canada; it is referred to as a "Vancouver Fog" in Scotland.[[141]](#cite_note-145)

The [Okanagan](/wiki/Okanagan "Okanagan") produces many unique fruits originating from the region, including [Ambrosia](/wiki/Ambrosia_apple "Ambrosia apple") and [Spartan](/wiki/Spartan_apple "Spartan apple") apples, [Stella](/wiki/Stella_cherry "Stella cherry") and [Skeena](/wiki/Skeena_cherry "Skeena cherry") cherries, and [Corontation](/wiki/Coronation_(grape) "Coronation (grape)") grapes. Other fruits grown in the province include [peaches](/wiki/Peaches "Peaches"), [pears](/wiki/Pears "Pears"), [plums](/wiki/Plums "Plums"), [apricots](/wiki/Apricots "Apricots"), [strawberries](/wiki/Strawberries "Strawberries"), [blackberries](/wiki/Blackberries "Blackberries"), [cranberries](/wiki/Cranberries "Cranberries"), and [loganberries](/wiki/Loganberries "Loganberries").[[140]](#cite_note-British_Columbia_Food_and_Drink-144)

British Columbia is renowned for its wine production. The primary wine-producing regions include the Okanagan, the [Similkameen Valley](/wiki/Similkameen_Valley "Similkameen Valley"), Vancouver Island, the [Gulf Islands](/wiki/Gulf_Islands "Gulf Islands"), and the [Fraser Valley](/wiki/Fraser_Valley "Fraser Valley"). As of November 2014, there are 280 licensed grape wineries and 929 vineyards.[[142]](#cite_note-146)

## Outdoor life and athletics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Whistler_ice_sailor.jpg/250px-Whistler_ice_sailor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whistler_ice_sailor.jpg)

Ice sailing in [Whistler](/wiki/Whistler,_British_Columbia "Whistler, British Columbia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Holland_Point_Shoreline_Trail%2C_Victoria%2C_British_Columbia%2C_Canada_05.jpg/250px-Holland_Point_Shoreline_Trail%2C_Victoria%2C_British_Columbia%2C_Canada_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holland_Point_Shoreline_Trail,_Victoria,_British_Columbia,_Canada_05.jpg)

Shoreline Trail in Victoria

Given its varied mountainous terrain and its coasts, lakes, rivers, and forests, British Columbia has long been enjoyed for pursuits like hiking and camping, [rock climbing](/wiki/Rock_climbing "Rock climbing") and mountaineering, hunting and fishing.

Water sports, both motorized and non-motorized, are enjoyed in many places. [Sea kayaking](/wiki/Sea_kayak "Sea kayak") opportunities abound on the British Columbia coast with its [fjords](/wiki/Fjord "Fjord"). [Whitewater rafting](/wiki/Whitewater_rafting "Whitewater rafting") and [kayaking](/wiki/Kayak "Kayak") are popular on many inland rivers. Sailing and [sailboarding](/wiki/Windsurfing "Windsurfing") are widely enjoyed.

In winter, [cross-country](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing "Cross-country skiing") and [telemark skiing](/wiki/Telemark_skiing "Telemark skiing") are much enjoyed, and in recent decades high-quality [downhill skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing "Alpine skiing") has been developed in the Coast Mountain range and the Rockies, as well as in the southern areas of the Shuswap Highlands and the [Columbia Mountains](/wiki/Columbia_Mountains "Columbia Mountains"). [Snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding "Snowboarding") has mushroomed in popularity since the early 1990s. The [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") downhill events were held in the [Whistler Blackcomb](/wiki/Whistler_Blackcomb "Whistler Blackcomb") area of the province, while the indoor events were conducted in the Vancouver area.

In Vancouver and Victoria (as well as some other cities), opportunities for [joggers](/wiki/Jogging "Jogging") and bicyclists have been developed. Cross-country bike touring has been popular since the [ten-speed](/wiki/Bicycle_gearing "Bicycle gearing") bike became available many years ago. Since the advent of the more robust [mountain bike](/wiki/Mountain_bike "Mountain bike"), trails in more rugged and wild places have been developed for them. A 2016 poll on global biking website Pinkbike rated BC as the top destination mountain bikers would like to ride.[[143]](#cite_note-147) Some of the province's retired rail beds have been converted and maintained for hiking, biking, and cross-country skiing. [Longboarding](/wiki/Longboarding "Longboarding") is also a popular activity because of the hilly geography of the region.

In certain areas, there are businesses, non-profit societies, or municipal governments dedicated to promoting [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism") in their region. A number of British Columbia farmers offer visitors to combine tourism with farm work, for example, through the [WWOOF](/wiki/WWOOF "WWOOF") Canada program.[[144]](#cite_note-148)

## Education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Hatley_Castle_BC.jpg/250px-Hatley_Castle_BC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hatley_Castle_BC.jpg)

[Hatley Castle](/wiki/Hatley_Castle "Hatley Castle") on the campus of [Royal Roads University](/wiki/Royal_Roads_University "Royal Roads University")

### K-12 education

See also: [Education in British Columbia](/wiki/Education_in_British_Columbia "Education in British Columbia")

British Columbia is home to a comprehensive education system consisting of public schools and independent schools that is overseen by the provincial Ministry of Education. The public school system is divided in 59 anglophone school districts and one francophone school district, the *[Conseil scolaire francophone de la Colombie-Britannique](/wiki/Conseil_scolaire_francophone_de_la_Colombie-Britannique "Conseil scolaire francophone de la Colombie-Britannique"),* which operates French-language public schools throughout the province.[[145]](#cite_note-149) The anglophone school districts are governed by school board trustees who are directly elected by the school district's residents. Although 86 percent of students are enrolled in the public school system, British Columbia has one of the highest shares of independent school enrolment among Canadian province, at 14 percent of the student population, due to its relatively generous funding model; most independent schools receive 50 percent of the operating funding that their public counterparts receive from the government. A very small percentage (less than 1 percent) of students are home schooled.

Like most other provinces in Canada, education is compulsory from ages 6 to 16 (grades 1–10), although the vast majority of students remain in school until they graduate from high school ([grade 12](/wiki/Twelfth_grade "Twelfth grade")) at the age of 18. In order to graduate with a graduation certificate, known as a Dogwood Diploma in BC, students must take a minimum of 80 course credits during grades 10 to 12. These credits include a variety of required courses (e.g. in language arts, social studies, mathematics, and science), as well as elective courses.[[146]](#cite_note-150)

Academic achievement in British Columbia is relatively good, although it has been slipping in recent years by some measures. In 2020, 86 percent of students in British Columbia graduated from high school within six years of entering grade 8.[[147]](#cite_note-151) According to the 2018 Programme for International Student Assessment (PISA) scores, students in British Columbia scored the second highest in reading ability, fourth highest in mathematic prowess, and fourth highest in science knowledge of the 10 Canadian provinces, although these scores have declined significantly since the 2000 and 2015 assessments.[[148]](#cite_note-152)

#### International students

In September 2014, there were 11,000 international students in BC public K-12 schools and about 3,000 international students in other BC K-12 schools.[[149]](#cite_note-153)

### Higher education

See also: [Higher education in British Columbia](/wiki/Higher_education_in_British_Columbia "Higher education in British Columbia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Quest_University_Canada_Aerial_View.jpg/250px-Quest_University_Canada_Aerial_View.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quest_University_Canada_Aerial_View.jpg)

Quest University Canada Academic Building, aerial view

British Columbia has a diverse array of higher educational institutions, ranging from publicly funded universities, colleges, and institutes, to private universities, colleges, seminaries, and career institutes. Public institutions receive approximately half of their funding from grants from the provincial government, with the remaining revenue stemming from tuition charges and philanthropic donations.[[150]](#cite_note-154) Each post-secondary institution sets its own admission requirements, although the standard requirement is the completion of high school.

Public universities and colleges include:

* [Simon Fraser University](/wiki/Simon_Fraser_University "Simon Fraser University")

* [Vancouver Island University](/wiki/Vancouver_Island_University "Vancouver Island University")
* [British Columbia Institute of Technology](/wiki/British_Columbia_Institute_of_Technology "British Columbia Institute of Technology")
